FILE: README.md
================================================
# Colibri Documentation System

This site documents Colibri, a framework-agnostic component library for building web UIs in JavaScript/Typescript. Colibri was designed and is maintained by [Telesign](https://www.telesign.com/).

Our documentation system is built using Storybook, providing an interactive environment for documenting components, design guidelines, and usage patterns.

## Table of Contents

- [Overview](#overview)
- [Getting Started](#getting-started)
- [Documentation Structure](#documentation-structure)
- [Writing Documentation](#writing-documentation)
- [Development](#development)
- [Customization](#customization)
- [Best Practices](#best-practices)
- [Contributing](#contributing)

## Overview

### Key Features

- Interactive component playground
- Web Components support
- Accessibility testing
- Responsive preview
- Theme switching
- Code snippets
- Framework integration guides

## Getting Started

### Installation

```bash
# Install dependencies
npm install

# Start development server
npm run dev

# Build documentation
npm run build
```

### Environment Setup

The documentation system requires:

- Node.js (latest LTS)
- npm or yarn
- Modern browser for development

### Component Registration

All web components must be registered in `.storybook/preview.ts` before they can be used in stories:

```typescript
// .storybook/preview.ts
import { registerColibriComponents, registerAllComponents } from '@telesign/colibri';
import { ColIcon } from '@telesign/colibri-icons';
import '@telesign/colibri/styles/styles.css';

// Register all components from @telesign/colibri
registerAllComponents();

// Register external components like icons separately
registerColibriComponents([ColIcon]);
```

This ensures components are available globally across all stories. Never register components in individual story files.

## Documentation Structure

```
colibri-docs/
├── src/
│   ├── assets/          # Static assets
│   ├── constants/       # Shared constants
│   ├── styles/          # Global styles
│   └── stories/         # Component documentation
│       └── components/  # Component stories
└── .storybook/         # Storybook configuration
    ├── preview.ts      # Global configuration and component registration
    └── main.ts         # Storybook configuration
```

## Writing Documentation

### Story File Organization

All story files should follow this standard organization:

#### 1. Imports

- External dependencies first
- Local imports second

#### 2. Interfaces/Types

- Story-specific type definitions
- Props interfaces

#### 3. Story Metadata (default export)

- Component documentation
- Props configuration (argTypes)
- Default args
- Parameters (including `__sb` for layout)

#### 4. Styles

- Story-specific styles using lit's css
- Placed before stories for context

#### 5. Stories

- Individual story implementations
- Story-specific documentation

For a complete example of this organization, see [ColIcon.stories.tsx](src/stories/components/ColIcon.stories.tsx). This story demonstrates:

- Proper file structure
- Component documentation
- Props configuration
- Layout customization
- Multiple story variants
- Styled components

### Component Usage Example

After registering a component in `preview.ts`, you can use it in your stories:

```typescript
// ComponentName.stories.ts
export const Default = () => html`
  <col-icon name="home" size="24px" color="currentColor"></col-icon>
`;
```

### Source Code Customization

Control how source code appears in your documentation with these options:

#### Excluding Decorators

To exclude decorators and show only the component code:

```typescript
parameters: {
  docs: {
    source: {
      excludeDecorators: true;
    }
  }
}
```

#### Transforming Source Code

To remove specific elements (like `<style>` tags) from the displayed source code:

```typescript
parameters: {
  docs: {
    source: {
      transform: (code: string): string => {
        // Remove style tags and their content
        return code.replace(/<style>[\s\S]*?<\/style>\s*/, '');
      };
    }
  }
}
```

#### Disabling Source Code Display

To hide the source code for specific stories:

```typescript
parameters: {
  docs: {
    source: {
      code: null; // Disables the "Show code" option
    }
  }
}
```

For a complete example of source code customization, see [ColIcon.stories.tsx](src/stories/components/ColIcon.stories.tsx).

### Layout Configuration

Stories can be configured with custom layouts using the `__sb` parameter:

```typescript
parameters: {
  __sb: {
    display: 'grid' | 'flex',
    gridTemplateColumns?: string,
    gap?: string,
    flexDirection?: 'row' | 'row-reverse' | 'column' | 'column-reverse',
    justifyContent?: 'flex-start' | 'flex-end' | 'center' | 'space-between' | 'space-around' | 'space-evenly',
    flexWrap?: 'nowrap' | 'wrap' | 'wrap-reverse'
  }
}
```

### Story Naming Convention

- Files should be placed in the `stories/components` directory
- File name should be `ComponentName.stories.ts`
- Story title should follow `Category/ComponentName` format

Available `Category` values:

- `Atoms`
- `Molecules`
- `Organisms`
- `Tokens`

### Example Story Component

A story file for a molecule component is available for reference at [ExampleTextField.stories.tsx](src/stories/components/ExampleTextField.stories.ts?at=refs%2Fheads%2Fshowcase%2Fstorybook-examples).

This file demonstrates:

- Usage of a molecule component (with slots and nested elements)
- Custom tokens usage for styling (e.g. colors.border.default)
- A custom transform function to clean up displayed source code
- Story structure and props setup following best practices

## Development

### Available Scripts

```json
{
  "dev": "storybook dev -p 6006",
  "build": "storybook build"
}
```

### Storybook Addons

Installed addons include:

- @storybook/addon-essentials
- @storybook/addon-a11y
- @storybook/addon-links

## Customization

### Story Organization

Stories are organized in a specific order:

1. Welcome
2. Atoms
3. Molecules
4. Organisms
5. Tokens

This ordering is configured in the preview configuration:

```typescript
options: {
  storySort: {
    order: ['Welcome', 'Atoms', 'Molecules', 'Organisms', 'Tokens'],
  }
}
```

### Theme Customization

The system supports theme switching through the `data-theme` attribute:

```typescript
decorators: [
  (story, context) => html` <div data-theme=${context.args.mode || 'telesign'}>${story()}</div> `,
];
```

## Best Practices

### Documentation Guidelines

1. Component Documentation

- Overview
- Props/API
- Examples
- Best practices
- Accessibility considerations

2. Code Examples

- Basic usage
- Common patterns
- Edge cases

3. Writing Style

- Clear and concise
- Consistent terminology
- Progressive disclosure
- Include visual examples

### Accessibility

1. Testing

- Use a11y addon
- Test with screen readers
- Keyboard navigation
- Color contrast

2. Documentation

- ARIA roles
- Keyboard interactions
- Screen reader behavior

## Contributing

### Documentation Updates

1. Fork the repository
2. Create feature branch
3. Make changes following the story organization guidelines
4. Submit pull request

### Review Process

1. Documentation review
2. Technical review
3. Accessibility review
4. Final approval

### Style Guide

1. File Organization

- Follow standard story file structure
- Use consistent naming
- Maintain clear hierarchy

2. Code Examples

- Follow style guide
- Include comments
- Show best practices

## Next Steps

After setup:

1. Review existing documentation
2. Identify gaps
3. Plan documentation structure
4. Create contribution guidelines
5. Set up automated deployment

import globals from 'globals';
import eslint from '@eslint/js';
import tseslint from '@typescript-eslint/eslint-plugin';
import typescript from '@typescript-eslint/parser';
import prettier from 'eslint-plugin-prettier';

export default [
  eslint.configs.recommended,
  {
    files: ['src/**/*.{ts,tsx}'],
    languageOptions: {
      parser: typescript,
      ecmaVersion: 2020,
      sourceType: 'module',
      globals: {
        ...globals.browser,
        HTMLElement: 'readonly',
        HTMLStyleElement: 'readonly',
        HTMLInputElement: 'readonly',
        HTMLFormElement: 'readonly',
        HTMLPreElement: 'readonly',
        FormData: 'readonly',
        MediaQueryList: 'readonly',
        MediaQueryListEvent: 'readonly',
        CSSStyleSheet: 'readonly',
        FocusOptions: 'readonly',
        EventListener: 'readonly',
        ElementInternals: 'readonly',
        requestAnimationFrame: 'readonly',
      },
    },
    plugins: {
      '@typescript-eslint': tseslint,
      prettier: prettier,
    },
    rules: {
      'prettier/prettier': 'error',
      '@typescript-eslint/explicit-function-return-type': 'off',
      '@typescript-eslint/no-explicit-any': 'warn',
      '@typescript-eslint/no-unused-vars': ['warn', { argsIgnorePattern: '^_', varsIgnorePattern: '^_' }],
      'no-unused-vars': 'off',
    },
  },
];

FILE: src/custom.d.ts
================================================
declare module '*.png' {
  const content: string;
  export default content;
}

declare module '*.jpg' {
  const content: string;
  export default content;
}

declare module '*.jpeg' {
  const content: string;
  export default content;
}

declare module '*.module.css' {
  const classes: { [key: string]: string };
  export default classes;
}



================================================
FILE: src/_storybook/react.mdx
================================================
import { Meta } from '@storybook/blocks';
import { Callout } from './components';

<Meta title="Framework Integration/React" />

# React Integration

This guide shows you how to integrate Colibri Web Components into React applications using two proven approaches: direct usage for simple cases, and React wrappers for enhanced developer experience.

## Table of Contents

- [Integration Overview](#integration-overview)
- [Setup and Configuration](#setup-and-configuration)
- [Components Usage](#components-usage)
- [Components with Slots](#components-with-slots)
- [Property Mapping](#property-mapping)
- [Component Composition Example](#component-composition-example)
- [Form Integration Example with Validation](#form-integration-example-with-validation)

## Integration Overview

Colibri components work in React out of the box, but React's architecture requires specific patterns for optimal integration. We'll cover both approaches so you can choose what works best for your project needs.

## Setup and Configuration

### Prerequisites

- React 18+ application
- TypeScript (recommended)

### Installation

Install the required packages:

- `@telesign/colibri` - Core Colibri components
- `@telesign/colibri-icons` - Colibri icon components
- `@lit/react` - React integration for Lit-based web components. For more details, see the <a href="https://lit.dev/docs/frameworks/react" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">Lit React integration docs</a>

```bash
npm install @telesign/colibri @telesign/colibri-icons @lit/react
```

### Components Registration

To register Colibri components in your React app, you have two options:

#### Option A: Register All Components (Recommended for most apps)

    ```typescript
    // src/main.tsx
    import { registerAllComponents } from '@telesign/colibri';
    import '@telesign/colibri/styles/styles.css';
    import { StrictMode } from 'react';
    import { createRoot } from 'react-dom/client';
    import App from './App';

    registerAllComponents();

    createRoot(document.getElementById('root')!).render(
      <StrictMode>
        <App />
      </StrictMode>
    );
    ```

    **Benefits:**
    - Simplest setup - one function call
    - All components available throughout your app
    - No need to track which components you're using
    - Best for apps that will use multiple Colibri components

#### Option B: Register Specific Components (For smaller bundles)

<Callout variant="info" icon="ℹ️">
  Remember that Colibri components have peer dependencies on each other. However, registering a
  component automatically registers its dependencies. For more details, please refer to the
  component overview documentation pages.
</Callout>

    ```typescript
    // src/main.tsx
    import { registerColibriComponents, ColButton, ColTextField } from '@telesign/colibri';
    import { ColIcon } from '@telesign/colibri-icons';
    import '@telesign/colibri/styles/styles.css';
    import { StrictMode } from 'react';
    import { createRoot } from 'react-dom/client';
    import App from './App';

    registerColibriComponents([ColButton, ColTextField, ColIcon]);

    createRoot(document.getElementById('root')!).render(
      <StrictMode>
        <App />
      </StrictMode>
    );
    ```

    **Benefits:**
    - Smaller bundle size - only includes components you use
    - More explicit about dependencies
    - Better for apps with strict performance requirements
    - Allows for gradual adoption

### Theme Management

Colibri supports theming through the `data-theme` attribute. To enable theming in your application:

1. Include the Colibri styles in your app:

   ```typescript
   // src/main.tsx
   import '@telesign/colibri/styles/styles.css';
   ```

2. Create a `ThemeProvider` component to manage themes in your app:

   ```typescript
     import React from 'react';

     export interface ThemeProviderProps {
       theme?: 'telesign' | 'massive' | 'bics';
       children: React.ReactNode;
     }

     export const ThemeProvider: React.FC<ThemeProviderProps> = ({ theme = 'telesign', children }) => {
       return <div data-theme={theme}>{children}</div>;
     };
   ```

3. Wrap your application with the `ThemeProvider`:

   ```typescript
     // src/App.tsx
     import React from 'react';
     import { ThemeProvider } from './components/ThemeProvider';
     import { YourAppComponents } from './YourAppComponents';

     const App: React.FC = () => {
       return (
         <ThemeProvider theme="telesign">
           <YourAppComponents />
         </ThemeProvider>
       );
     };

     export default App;
   ```

## Components Usage

In order to use Colibri components in your React application, you have two main approaches:

### 1. Direct Usage (Simplest)

You can use Colibri web components directly in React JSX. This approach works but has limitations with TypeScript integration and event handling:

```typescript
import React, { useState } from 'react';

function MyComponent() {
  const [username, setUsername] = useState('');

  const handleInput = (event: Event) => {
    const target = event.target as HTMLInputElement;
    setUsername(target.value);
  };

  return (
    <div>
      <col-text-field
        label="Username"
        name="username"
        value={username}
        onInput={handleInput}
        placeholder="Enter your username"
      />
    </div>
  );
}
```

**Limitations of Direct Usage:**

- Limited TypeScript autocomplete and type checking
- Manual event handling with refs for complex components
- Property vs attribute handling issues
- No camelCase prop support

### 2. React Wrappers (Recommended)

For a better developer experience, create React wrapper components using `@lit/react`. This approach provides full TypeScript support, React-style event handling, and camelCase props.

<Callout variant="info" icon="ℹ️">
  For more details, see the{' '}
  <a href="https://lit.dev/docs/frameworks/react" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">
    Lit React integration docs
  </a>
  .
</Callout>

<Callout variant="tip" icon="💡">
  For the remainder of this documentation, we will use this approach as the basis for all further
  examples and explanations.
</Callout>

For components without custom events:

```tsx
import React from 'react';
import { createComponent } from '@lit/react';
import { ColAvatar } from '@telesign/colibri';

export const Avatar = createComponent({
  tagName: 'col-avatar',
  elementClass: ColAvatar,
  react: React,
});
```

For components that emit custom events:

<Callout variant="info" icon="ℹ️">
  For more details on event handling, see the{' '}
  <a href="https://lit.dev/docs/frameworks/react/#events" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">
    Lit React events docs
  </a>
  .
</Callout>

```tsx
import React from 'react';
import { createComponent } from '@lit/react';
import { ColDialog } from '@telesign/colibri';

export const Dialog = createComponent({
  tagName: 'col-dialog',
  elementClass: ColDialog,
  react: React,
  events: {
    onOpen: 'open',
    onClose: 'close',
    onCancel: 'cancel',
  },
});
```

## Components with Slots

Colibri components often use slots for flexible content insertion. For using slots in React you have two options depending on the type of slot you need to fill:

### Default Slots

For components with a single default slot, you can pass children directly. In the example below, the `col-button` component uses its default slot for the button label:

```tsx
import React from 'react';
import { Button } from './components/Button';
import { BUTTON_VARIANTS, COLORS } from '@telesign/colibri';

<Button variant={BUTTON_VARIANTS.DEFAULT} color={COLORS.PRIMARY} onClick={onClick}>
  Default Button
</Button>;
```

**How to know if a component has a default slot?**

Check the component overview documentation page for slot details. Typically, if a component has a single unnamed slot, it can be filled by passing children. In the code snippet from the documentation you could see something like this (**Lit HTML version**):

```html
<col-button>
  Text
  <!-- This text goes into the default slot -->
</col-button>
```

### Named Slots

For components with named slots, use the `slot` attribute on child elements to specify which slot they should fill. In the example below, the `col-badge` component has an "icon" named slot:

```tsx
import React, { useState } from 'react';
import { Badge } from './components/Badge';
import { BADGE_VARIANTS } from '@telesign/colibri';

<Badge variant={BADGE_VARIANTS.DEFAULT}>
  <div slot="icon" style={{ display: 'contents' }}>
    <Icon name="emoji-circle" size="12" />
  </div>
  Default Badge
</Badge>;
```

**How to know if a component has a named slot?**

Check the component overview documentation page for slot details. Named slots will be indicated with a `slot` attribute in the code snippet from the documentation, like this (**Lit HTML version**):

```html
<col-badge variant="default">
  <col-icon slot="icon" size="12px" name="emoji-circle">
    <!-- This icon goes into the "icon" named slot -->
  </col-icon>
  Text
  <!-- This text goes into the default slot -->
</col-badge>
```

**Key points:**

- Create a wrapper and use the `slot` attribute to target the named slot.
- The wrapper can be any HTML element, but a `div` is commonly used.
- For better layout control, especially with inline elements, consider wrapping the slotted content in a `div` with `display: contents` style.
- For more details, see the <a href="https://lit.dev/docs/frameworks/react/#using-slots" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">Lit slotted content docs</a>.

## Property Mapping

A key advantage of React wrappers is that they allow you to use familiar React conventions instead of the standard HTML patterns.

### Component Documentation vs React Usage

**Storybook/Documentation Examples (Lit HTML)**:

```html
<!-- kebab-case attributes in HTML -->
<col-text-field input-type="password" max-length="20" error-message="Invalid input">
</col-text-field>
```

**React Wrapper Usage (JavaScript Properties)**:

```jsx
{
  /* camelCase props in React */
}
<TextField inputType="password" maxLength={20} errorMessage="Invalid input" />;
```

### Why This Works

React wrappers set properties directly on the web component's JavaScript object, bypassing HTML attributes:

- **Documentation**: Shows HTML attributes (`input-type`) that map to properties
- **React Wrappers**: Set JavaScript properties directly (`inputType`)
- **Result**: Same functionality, different syntax conventions

This allows React developers to follow React conventions while maintaining full compatibility with Colibri components.

## Component Composition Example

Colibri's flexible component architecture allows you to compose complex UIs by combining simple building blocks. For example, you can easily create a custom select component by composing Colibri components together:

### `Select` Component with `ListMenu`

Here's how to use Select components with React wrappers in your application:

```tsx
import React, { useState } from 'react';
import { Select, ListMenu, ListMenuItem } from './components';

function SelectExample() {
  const [selectedValue, setSelectedValue] = useState('');

  const handleChange = (event: CustomEvent) => {
    setSelectedValue(event.detail.value);
    console.log('Selected:', event.detail.value);
  };

  return (
    <div>
      <Select
        label="Choose an option"
        placeholder="Select an option"
        value={selectedValue}
        onChange={handleChange}
        onFocus={e => console.log('Select focused')}
        onBlur={e => console.log('Select blurred')}
      >
        <ListMenu>
          <ListMenuItem value="option1">Option 1</ListMenuItem>
          <ListMenuItem value="option2">Option 2</ListMenuItem>
          <ListMenuItem value="option3">Option 3</ListMenuItem>
        </ListMenu>
      </Select>

      {/* Disabled state example */}
      <Select label="Disabled Select" disabled onChange={handleChange}>
        <ListMenu>
          <ListMenuItem value="disabled">This is disabled</ListMenuItem>
        </ListMenu>
      </Select>
    </div>
  );
}
```

## Form Integration Example with Validation

Colibri Web Components are designed to work seamlessly with React forms, supporting both controlled and uncontrolled patterns. Here's how to create a complete form with validation in React:

```tsx
import React, { useState } from 'react';
import { TextField, TextArea, Select, Button, ListMenu, ListMenuItem } from './components';
import { BUTTON_VARIANTS } from '@telesign/colibri';

function InteractiveFormExample() {
  const [formData, setFormData] = useState({
    username: '',
    email: '',
    password: '',
    confirmPassword: '',
    country: '',
    bio: '',
  });

  const handleSubmit = (event: React.FormEvent) => {
    event.preventDefault();

    // Colibri's form-based components automatically handle validation
    // If validation passes, this code will execute
    console.log('Form submitted successfully:', formData);

    // You can access form data using FormData API
    const formDataObj = new FormData(event.target as HTMLFormElement);
    const formValues = Object.fromEntries(formDataObj);
    console.log('Form values:', formValues);
  };

  const updateField = (field: string) => (event: Event) => {
    const target = event.target as HTMLInputElement;
    setFormData(prev => ({
      ...prev,
      [field]: target.value,
    }));
  };

  return (
    <form onSubmit={handleSubmit} style={{ maxWidth: '500px', margin: '0 auto' }}>
      <h3>Registration Form</h3>

      {/* Required text field with validation */}
      <TextField
        label="Username"
        name="username"
        value={formData.username}
        onInput={updateField('username')}
        placeholder="Enter your username"
        required
        minLength={3}
        maxLength={20}
      />

      {/* Email field with validation */}
      <TextField
        label="Email"
        name="email"
        inputType="email"
        value={formData.email}
        onInput={updateField('email')}
        placeholder="Enter your email"
        required
      />

      {/* Password field */}
      <TextField
        label="Password"
        name="password"
        inputType="password"
        value={formData.password}
        onInput={updateField('password')}
        placeholder="Enter your password"
        required
        minLength={8}
        subLabel="Minimum 8 characters"
      />

      {/* Confirm password with custom validation */}
      <TextField
        label="Confirm Password"
        name="confirmPassword"
        inputType="password"
        value={formData.confirmPassword}
        onInput={updateField('confirmPassword')}
        placeholder="Confirm your password"
        required
        error={formData.confirmPassword !== '' && formData.password !== formData.confirmPassword}
        errorMessage={formData.password !== formData.confirmPassword ? "Passwords don't match" : ''}
      />

      {/* Select dropdown */}
      <Select
        label="Country"
        name="country"
        placeholder="Select your country"
        required
        onChange={(event: CustomEvent) => {
          setFormData(prev => ({ ...prev, country: event.detail.value }));
        }}
      >
        <ListMenu>
          <ListMenuItem value="us">United States</ListMenuItem>
          <ListMenuItem value="ca">Canada</ListMenuItem>
          <ListMenuItem value="uk">United Kingdom</ListMenuItem>
          <ListMenuItem value="au">Australia</ListMenuItem>
        </ListMenu>
      </Select>

      {/* Text area */}
      <TextArea
        label="Bio"
        name="bio"
        value={formData.bio}
        onInput={updateField('bio')}
        placeholder="Tell us about yourself"
        maxLength={500}
        charCount={500}
      />

      {/* Submit button */}
      <Button
        type="submit"
        variant={BUTTON_VARIANTS.DEFAULT}
        style={{ marginTop: '20px', width: '100%' }}
      >
        Register
      </Button>
    </form>
  );
}
```

### Key Form Integration Points

**Automatic Validation**: Colibri components use a custom form validation logic which automatically handles validation failures:

- Validates all form components when the form is submitted
- **Prevents submission** if any component is invalid (calls `event.preventDefault()`)
- **Focuses the first invalid field** to guide user attention
- Works with HTML5 validation attributes (`required`, `minLength`, `maxLength`, `pattern`)
- Only intervenes on validation failures - successful validation allows normal form submission

**Event Handling**: Form components emit both standard HTML events (`input`, `change`) and custom events with additional data in `event.detail`.

**Controlled Components**: Use React state to control component values while letting Colibri handle validation failure scenarios and form submission behavior.

**FormData Integration**: Colibri components work seamlessly with the FormData API for form submission handling when validation passes.

<Subtitle>Quick Start</Subtitle>

Install the package:

```bash
npm install @telesign/colibri @telesign/colibri-icons
```

```typescript
// Import libraries
import { registerColibriComponents, registerAllComponents, ColButton, ColCard } from '@telesign/colibri';
import { ColIcon } from '@telesign/colibri-icons';
import '@telesign/colibri/styles/styles.css';

// Option 1: Register specific components
registerColibriComponents([ColButton, ColCard]);

// Option 2: Register all components from @telesign/colibri
registerAllComponents();

// Always register external components like icons separately
registerColibriComponents([ColIcon]);

// Add data-theme attribute on root - parent element
<div data-theme="telesign">

// Use components
<col-icon name="home"></col-icon>
```

Now you're ready to use the ColButton component:

```html
<div data-theme="telesign">
  <col-button color="primary">
    Send
    <col-icon name="send"></col-icon>
  </col-button>
</div>
```

- 🔧 **Customizable**: Style tokens and CSS custom properties
- ♿ **Accessible**: Built with accessibility in mind
- 📱 **Responsive**: Mobile-first approach
- ⚡ **Performant**: Lightweight and fast
- 🛠️ **Web Components**: Framework agnostic

<Subtitle>Browser Support</Subtitle>

Colibri components work in all modern browsers:

- Chrome/Edge ≥ 79
- Firefox ≥ 67
- Safari ≥ 14
- iOS Safari ≥ 14

FILE: src/_storybook/components/Callout/Callout.tsx
================================================
import React from 'react';
import styles from './Callout.module.css';

export interface CalloutProps {
  variant: 'info' | 'tip' | 'warning' | 'error';
  icon?: string;
  children: React.ReactNode;
}

/**
 * A React component that renders a styled callout box for highlighting important information in Storybook documentation
 *
 * Note: This is a React-based documentation component, not a Lit-based Colibri web component.
 * Can be used in: MDX files only (not compatible with TSX files configured for Lit template rendering)
 *
 * @example
 * ```mdx
 * import { Callout } from '@/_storybook/components';
 *
 * <Callout variant="info" icon="ℹ️">
 *   This is an informational message.
 * </Callout>
 *
 * <Callout variant="warning" icon="⚠️">
 *   <p>Warning: This action cannot be undone.</p>
 * </Callout>
 *
 * <Callout variant="tip" icon="💡">
 *   Pro tip: Use dynamic links for better maintainability.
 * </Callout>
 * ```
 */
export const Callout: React.FC<CalloutProps> = ({ variant, icon, children }) => {
  return (
    <div className={`${styles.callout} ${styles[variant]}`}>
      {icon && <div className={styles.icon}>{icon}</div>}
      <div className={styles.content}>{children}</div>
    </div>
  );
};

FILE: src/_storybook/components/Card/Card.tsx
================================================
import React from 'react';
import styles from './Card.module.css';

export interface CardProps {
  title: string;
  children: React.ReactNode;
  variant?: 'default' | 'outlined' | 'elevated';
  size?: 'sm' | 'md' | 'lg';
  className?: string;
}

/**
 * A React component for organizing and displaying content in card layouts within Storybook documentation
 *
 * Note: This is a React-based documentation component, not a Lit-based Colibri web component.
 * Can be used in: MDX files only (not compatible with TSX files configured for Lit template rendering)
 *
 * @example
 * ```mdx
 * import { Card } from '@/_storybook/components';
 *
 * <Card title="Example Card" variant="outlined" size="md">
 *   <p>This is the card content.</p>
 * </Card>
 *
 * <Card title="Elevated Card" variant="elevated">
 *   <ul>
 *     <li>Item 1</li>
 *     <li>Item 2</li>
 *   </ul>
 * </Card>
 *
 * <Card title="Features" variant="default" size="lg">
 *   <p>Component features and capabilities</p>
 * </Card>
 * ```
 */
export const Card: React.FC<CardProps> = ({
  title,
  children,
  variant = 'default',
  size = 'md',
  className = '',
}) => {
  const cardClasses = [styles.card, styles[variant], styles[size], className]
    .filter(Boolean)
    .join(' ');

  return (
    <div className={cardClasses}>
      <p className={styles.title}>{title}</p>
      <div className={styles.content}>{children}</div>
    </div>
  );
};

FILE: src/_storybook/components/CodeBlock/CodeBlock.ts
================================================
import { LitElement, html } from 'lit';
import { customElement, property } from 'lit/decorators.js';
import { unsafeHTML } from 'lit/directives/unsafe-html.js';
import { CodeBlockStyles } from './CodeBlock.styles';
import { highlightJsStyles } from './highlight-js.styles';

/**
 * A Lit-based web component for displaying syntax-highlighted code blocks in Storybook documentation
 *
 * Note: This is a Lit-based web component for use in story files (.stories.tsx), not a React component.
 * Can be used in: TSX story files (Lit template rendering)
 *
 * @example
 * ```typescript
 * import { html } from 'lit';
 * import '@/_storybook/components/CodeBlock';
 *
 * export const CodeExample = () => html`
 *   <code-block
 *     code="const example = 'Hello World';"
 *     language="javascript"
 *     code-theme="dark"
 *     title="JavaScript Example"
 *   ></code-block>
 * `;
 * ```
 */
@customElement('code-block')
export class CodeBlock extends LitElement {
  static styles = [CodeBlockStyles, highlightJsStyles];

  @property({ type: String })
  code = '';

  @property({ type: String, attribute: 'code-theme' })
  codeTheme = 'dark';

  @property({ type: String })
  language = '';

  @property({ type: String })
  title = '';

  render() {
    const codeClass = this.codeTheme === 'dark' ? 'storybook-code' : 'storybook-code light';

    return html`
      <div class="code-block-container">
        ${this.title ? html`<h3>${this.title}</h3>` : ''}
        <div class="${codeClass}">
          <pre><code class="${this.language ? `language-${this.language}` : ''}">${unsafeHTML(
            this.code
          )}</code></pre>
        </div>
      </div>
    `;
  }
}

declare global {
  interface HTMLElementTagNameMap {
    'code-block': CodeBlock;
  }
}

import { css } from 'lit';

/*

Atom One Dark by Daniel Gamage
Original One Dark Syntax theme from https://github.com/atom/one-dark-syntax
Based on the highlight.js theme: https://highlightjs.org/
To change the theme, update the colors in the css variables
There are some themes refefences in the `node_modules/highlight.js/styles/` folder.


base:    #282c34
mono-1:  #abb2bf
mono-2:  #818896
mono-3:  #5c6370
hue-1:   #56b6c2
hue-2:   #61aeee
hue-3:   #c678dd
hue-4:   #98c379
hue-5:   #e06c75
hue-5-2: #be5046
hue-6:   #d19a66
hue-6-2: #e6c07b

*/
export const highlightJsStyles = css`
  :host {
    /* Base colors */
    --hljs-base: #282c34;
    --hljs-mono-1: #abb2bf;
    --hljs-mono-2: #818896;
    --hljs-mono-3: #5c6370;

    --hljs-hue-4: #98c379;
    --hljs-hue-5: #e06c75;
    --hljs-hue-5-2: #be5046;
    --hljs-hue-6: #d19a66;
    --hljs-hue-6-2: #e6c07b;
  }

  pre code.hljs {
    display: block;
    overflow-x: auto;
    padding: 1em;
  }

  code.hljs {
    padding: 3px 5px;
  }

    color: var(--hljs-mono-1);

    text-decoration: underline;
  }
`;

FILE: src/_storybook/components/Component/Component.tsx
================================================
import React from 'react';
import styles from './Component.module.css';

export interface ComponentProps {
  text: string;
  variant?:
    | 'default'
    | 'custom'
    | 'tokens'
    | 'inline'
    | 'invalid'
    | 'adaptive'
    | 'conditional'
    | 'responsive'
    | 'composite';
  onClick?: () => void;
  disabled?: boolean;
  style?: React.CSSProperties;
  className?: string;
}

/**
 * A flexible React component for displaying component examples and states in Storybook documentation
 *
 * Note: This is a React-based documentation component, not a Lit-based Colibri web component.
 * Can be used in: MDX files only (not compatible with TSX files configured for Lit template rendering)
 *
 * @example
 * ```mdx
 * import { Component } from '@/_storybook/components';
 *
 * <Component text="Default Component" variant="default" />
 *
 * <Component
 *   text="Interactive Component"
 *   variant="custom"
 *   onClick={() => console.log('clicked')}
 * />
 *
 * <Component
 *   text="Disabled Component"
 *   variant="adaptive"
 *   disabled
 * />
 *
 * <Component
 *   text="Responsive Example"
 *   variant="responsive"
 *   onClick={() => alert('Clicked!')}
 * />
 * ```
 */
export const Component: React.FC<ComponentProps> = ({
  text,
  variant = 'default',
  onClick,
  disabled = false,
  style = {},
  className = '',
}) => {
  const componentClass = `${styles.component} ${styles[variant]} ${className}`;

  return (
    <div
      className={componentClass}
      onClick={onClick}
      style={{
        cursor: onClick ? 'pointer' : 'default',
        ...style,
      }}
      data-disabled={disabled}
    >
      {text}
    </div>
  );
};

FILE: src/_storybook/components/Container/Container.tsx
================================================
import React from 'react';
import styles from './Container.module.css';

export interface ContainerProps {
  title?: string;
  description?: string;
  children: React.ReactNode;
  variant?: 'default' | 'showcase' | 'example';
  className?: string;
  showHeader?: boolean;
}

/**
 * A React container component for wrapping and organizing content sections in Storybook documentation
 *
 * Note: This is a React-based documentation component, not a Lit-based Colibri web component.
 * Can be used in: MDX files only (not compatible with TSX files configured for Lit template rendering)
 *
 * @example
 * ```mdx
 * import { Container } from '@/_storybook/components';
 *
 * <Container title="Example Section" description="This is a description">
 *   <p>Content goes here</p>
 * </Container>
 *
 * <Container variant="showcase" showHeader={false}>
 *   <div>Content without header</div>
 * </Container>
 *
 * <Container
 *   title="Code Example"
 *   variant="example"
 *   description="Demonstrating the component usage"
 * >
 *   <pre><code>const example = true;</code></pre>
 * </Container>
 *
 * <Container title="API Reference" variant="default">
 *   <p>API documentation content</p>
 * </Container>
 * ```
 */
export const Container: React.FC<ContainerProps> = ({
  title,
  description,
  children,
  variant = 'default',
  className = '',
  showHeader = true,
}) => {
  const containerClass = `${styles.container} ${styles[variant]} ${className}`;

  return (
    <div className={containerClass}>
      {showHeader && (title || description) && (
        <div className={styles.header}>
          {title && <h5 className={styles.title}>{title}</h5>}
          {description && <p className={styles.description}>{description}</p>}
        </div>
      )}
      <div className={styles.content}>{children}</div>
    </div>
  );
};

================================================
FILE: src/_storybook/components/DocsLinkTo/DocsLinkTo.tsx
================================================
/* eslint-disable @typescript-eslint/no-explicit-any */
import type { MouseEvent, ReactNode } from 'react';
import React, { useEffect, useState, useCallback } from 'react';
import { navigate } from '@storybook/addon-links';
import { toId } from '@storybook/csf';

const LEFT_BUTTON = 0;

const isPlainLeftClick = (e: MouseEvent<HTMLAnchorElement>) =>
  e.button === LEFT_BUTTON && !e.altKey && !e.ctrlKey && !e.metaKey && !e.shiftKey;

const cancelled = (e: MouseEvent<HTMLAnchorElement>, cb = (_e: any) => {}) => {
  if (isPlainLeftClick(e)) {
    e.preventDefault();
    cb(e);
  }
};

interface DocsLinkToProps {
  title: string;
  children: ReactNode;
  [key: string]: any;
}

/**
 * A React component for creating dynamic navigation links to Storybook docs pages
 *
 * This component generates links to documentation pages based on story titles,
 * ensuring links remain valid even when story titles change. It uses Storybook's
 * internal navigation system for smooth client-side transitions.
 *
 * Note: This is a React-based documentation component, not a Lit-based Colibri web component.
 * Can be used in: MDX files only (not compatible with TSX files configured for Lit template rendering)
 *
 * @example
 * ```mdx
 * import { DocsLinkTo } from '@/_storybook/components';
 * import * as ComponentStories from './component.stories';
 *
 * <DocsLinkTo title="Foundation/Colors">See Colors Documentation</DocsLinkTo>
 *
 * <DocsLinkTo title={ComponentStories.default.title}>Component Docs</DocsLinkTo>
 *
 * <DocsLinkTo title="Components/Button" className="custom-link">
 *   Button Documentation
 * </DocsLinkTo>
 * ```
 */
export const DocsLinkTo: React.FC<DocsLinkToProps> = ({ title, children, ...rest }) => {
  const [href, setHref] = useState('/');

  const updateHref = useCallback(async () => {
    const id = toId(title);
    const path = `/docs/${id}`;
    const sbPath = window.location.pathname.replace(/iframe\.html$/, '');
    const url = `${window.location.origin}${sbPath}?path=${path}`;
    setHref(url);
  }, [title]);

  useEffect(() => {
    updateHref();
  }, [updateHref]);

  const handleClick = () => {
    const id = toId(title);
    navigate({ storyId: id });
  };

  return (
    <a href={href} onClick={e => cancelled(e, handleClick)} {...rest}>
      {children}
    </a>
  );
};

export * from './DocsLinkTo';

FILE: src/_storybook/components/FormDemo/FormDemo.ts
================================================
import { LitElement, html } from 'lit';
import { customElement, property, query, queryAssignedElements } from 'lit/decorators.js';
import { FormDemoStyles } from './FormDemo.styles';
import '@/_storybook/components/CodeBlock';

/**
 * A Lit-based web component for demonstrating form components with live output preview and code snippets
 *
 * Note: This is a Lit-based web component for use in story files (.stories.tsx), not a React component.
 * Can be used in: TSX story files (Lit template rendering)
 *
 * @example
 * ```typescript
 * import { html } from 'lit';
 * import '@/_storybook/components/FormDemo';
 *
 * export const FormExample = () => html`
 *   <form-demo
 *     form-id="example-form"
 *     output-id="example-output"
 *     title="Contact Form"
 *     form-description="A simple contact form example"
 *     code-snippet="const formData = new FormData(form);"
 *   >
 *     <form slot="form">
 *       <input type="text" name="name" placeholder="Name" />
 *       <button type="submit">Submit</button>
 *     </form>
 *   </form-demo>
 * `;
 * ```
 */
@customElement('form-demo')
export class FormDemo extends LitElement {
  static styles = [FormDemoStyles];

  @property({ type: String, attribute: 'form-id' })
  formId = '';

  @property({ type: String, attribute: 'output-id' })
  outputId = '';

  @property({ type: String })
  title = '';

  @property({ type: String, attribute: 'code-snippet' })
  codeSnippet = '';

  @property({ type: Boolean })
  showCodeBlock = true;

  @property({ type: Boolean })
  showOutput = true;

  @property({ type: String, attribute: 'code-theme' })
  codeTheme = 'dark';

  @property({ type: String })
  formDescription = '';

  @queryAssignedElements({ slot: 'form', flatten: true })
  private formElements!: HTMLElement[];

  @query(`[id$="-form-output"]`)
  private outputElement!: HTMLPreElement;

  private _handleSlotChange = () => {
    requestAnimationFrame(() => {
      this.setupFormHandlers();
    });
  };

  private setupFormHandlers() {
    const formElement = this.formElements?.[0] as HTMLFormElement;

    if (!formElement || !this.outputElement) return;

    formElement.removeEventListener('submit', this._handleSubmit);
    formElement.removeEventListener('reset', this._handleReset);

    formElement.addEventListener('submit', this._handleSubmit);
    formElement.addEventListener('reset', this._handleReset);
  }

  private _handleSubmit = (event: Event) => {
    if (event.defaultPrevented) {
      this.outputElement.textContent = 'Form submission blocked by validation';
      return;
    }

    event.preventDefault();

    const formData = new FormData(event.target as HTMLFormElement);
    const data = Object.fromEntries(formData.entries());
    this.outputElement.textContent = JSON.stringify(data, null, 2);
  };

  private _handleReset = () => {
    this.outputElement.textContent = 'Submit the form to see the data here';

    this.dispatchEvent(
      new CustomEvent('form-reset', {
        bubbles: true,
        composed: true,
      })
    );
  };

  render() {
    const isInDocs = window.location.search.includes('viewMode=docs');

    return html`
      <div class="storybook-card">
        ${!isInDocs
          ? html`
              <div class="storybook-flex">
                <div class="storybook-col">
                  <h3>${this.title}</h3>
                  ${this.formDescription ? html`<p>${this.formDescription}</p>` : ''}
                  <div>
                    <slot name="form" @slotchange=${this._handleSlotChange}></slot>
                  </div>
                </div>
                ${this.showOutput
                  ? html`
                      <div class="storybook-col">
                        <h3>Form Output</h3>
                        <pre class="form-output" id="${this.outputId}">
Submit the form to see the data here
                        </pre
                        >
                      </div>
                    `
                  : ''}
              </div>
              ${this.showCodeBlock && this.codeSnippet
                ? html`
                    <code-block
                      code="${this.codeSnippet}"
                      code-theme="${this.codeTheme}"
                      title="Form Data Integration in JavaScript"
                    ></code-block>
                  `
                : ''}
            `
          : html`
              <h3>${this.title}</h3>
              ${this.formDescription ? html`<p>${this.formDescription}</p>` : ''}
              <div>
                <slot name="form" @slotchange=${this._handleSlotChange}></slot>
              </div>
            `}
      </div>
    `;
  }
}

declare global {
  interface HTMLElementTagNameMap {
    'form-demo': FormDemo;
  }
}

FILE: src/_storybook/components/LayoutDemo/LayoutDemo.ts
================================================
import { LitElement, html } from 'lit';
import { customElement, property } from 'lit/decorators.js';
import { classMap } from 'lit/directives/class-map.js';
import { LayoutDemoStyles } from './LayoutDemo.styles';

/**
 * A Lit-based web component for demonstrating page layouts and content areas in Storybook
 *
 * Note: This is a Lit-based web component for use in story files (.stories.tsx), not a React component.
 * Can be used in: TSX story files (Lit template rendering)
 *
 * @example
 * ```typescript
 * import { html } from 'lit';
 * import '@/_storybook/components/LayoutDemo';
 *
 * export const LayoutExample = () => html`
 *   <layout-demo
 *     title="Dashboard Layout"
 *     show-placeholder
 *     placeholder-text="Your dashboard content goes here"
 *   ></layout-demo>
 *
 *   <layout-demo title="Custom Layout">
 *     <div>Custom content in the layout</div>
 *   </layout-demo>
 * `;
 * ```
 */
@customElement('layout-demo')
export class LayoutDemo extends LitElement {
  static styles = [LayoutDemoStyles];

  @property()
  title = 'Page Title';

  @property({ type: Boolean })
  showPlaceholder = false;

  @property({ type: String, attribute: 'placeholder-text' }) placeholderText =
    'Main page content goes here';

  render() {
    const contentClasses = {
      'content-area': true,
      'content-area--placeholder': this.showPlaceholder,
      'content-area--custom': !this.showPlaceholder,
    };

    return html`
      <div class="layout-wrapper">
        <h3 class="page-title">${this.title}</h3>
        <div class=${classMap(contentClasses)}>
          ${this.showPlaceholder
            ? html`<div class="page-content">${this.placeholderText}</div>`
            : html`<slot></slot>`}
        </div>
      </div>
    `;
  }
}

declare global {
  interface HTMLElementTagNameMap {
    'layout-demo': LayoutDemo;
  }
}

import { css } from 'lit';

export const MainLogoStyles = css`
  :host {
    display: inline-block;
    line-height: 0;
  }

  .logo-container {
    width: 100%;
    height: 100%;
    display: flex;
    align-items: center;
    justify-content: center;
  }

  .logo-container svg {
    display: block;
    width: 100%;
    height: 100%;
  }

  /* Default brand colors for each logo */
  .telesign-logo path {
    fill: var(--logo-telesign-color, #011ac2);
  }

  .massive-logo path {
    fill: var(--logo-massive-color, #1c85e8);
  }

  .bics-logo path {
    fill: var(--logo-bics-color, #2c3f46);
  }

  /* Site menu color variants override brand colors */
  :host([color='primary']) .logo-container svg path {
    fill: var(--logo-primary-color, #ffffff) !important;
  }

  :host([color='dark']) .logo-container svg path {
    fill: var(--logo-dark-color, #ffffff) !important;
  }

  :host([color='light']) .logo-container svg path {
    fill: var(--logo-light-color, #1a1a1a) !important;
  }

  /* Theme-specific colors - set via JavaScript */
  :host([data-current-theme='telesign']) {
    --logo-telesign-color: #011ac2;
    --logo-primary-color: #011ac2;
    --logo-dark-color: #ffffff;
    --logo-light-color: #011ac2;
  }

  :host([data-current-theme='massive']) {
    --logo-massive-color: #ffffff;
    --logo-primary-color: #ffffff;
    --logo-dark-color: #ffffff;
    --logo-light-color: #1c85e8;
  }

  :host([data-current-theme='bics']) {
    --logo-bics-color: #2c3f46;
    --logo-primary-color: #ffffff;
    --logo-dark-color: #ffffff;
    --logo-light-color: #2c3f46;
  }

  :host([style*='--custom-color']) .logo-container svg path {
    fill: var(--custom-color) !important;
  }
`;

FILE: src/_storybook/components/MainLogo/MainLogo.ts
================================================
import { html, LitElement, TemplateResult } from 'lit';
import { customElement, property, state } from 'lit/decorators.js';
import { unsafeHTML } from 'lit/directives/unsafe-html.js';
import { styleMap } from 'lit/directives/style-map.js';
import { MainLogoStyles } from './MainLogo.styles';

export type LogoName = 'telesign' | 'massive' | 'bics';
export type LogoColor = 'primary' | 'dark' | 'light';

/**
 * A Lit-based web component for displaying brand logos with theme-aware switching and dynamic sizing
 *
 * Note: This is a Lit-based web component for use in story files (.stories.tsx), not a React component.
 * Can be used in: TSX story files (Lit template rendering) and MDX files
 *
 * @example
 * ```typescript
 * // In TSX story files
 * import { html } from 'lit';
 * import '@/_storybook/components/MainLogo';
 *
 * export const LogoExample = () => html`
 *   <main-logo name="telesign" color="primary" size="200px"></main-logo>
 *   <main-logo name="massive" size="150x50"></main-logo>
 * `;
 * ```
 *
 * @example
 * ```mdx
 * // In MDX files
 * import '@/_storybook/components/MainLogo';
 *
 * <main-logo name="bics" color="dark" size="180px"></main-logo>
 * ```
 */
@customElement('main-logo')
export class MainLogo extends LitElement {
  static styles = MainLogoStyles;

  @property({ type: String })
  name?: LogoName;

  @property({ type: String })
  color?: LogoColor;

  @property({ type: String })
  size?: string;

  @state()
  private _svgContent: string = '';

  @state()
  private _loading: boolean = false;

  @state()
  private _error: boolean = false;

  private static _svgCache = new Map<string, string>();

  connectedCallback(): void {
    super.connectedCallback();
    this._updateLogoBasedOnTheme();
    this._observeThemeChanges();
  }

  updated(changedProperties: Map<string, unknown>): void {
    if (changedProperties.has('name')) {
      this._loadLogo();
    }
  }

  private _getEffectiveLogoName(): LogoName {
    if (this.name) {
      return this.name;
    }

    const theme = this._getThemeFromContext();
    switch (theme) {
      case 'massive':
        return 'massive';
      case 'bics':
        return 'bics';
      case 'telesign':
      default:
        return 'telesign';
    }
  }

  private _getThemeFromContext(): string {
    const storybook = document.querySelector('[data-theme]');
    if (storybook) {
      return storybook.getAttribute('data-theme') || 'telesign';
    }

    const body = document.body;
    if (body.classList.contains('theme-massive')) return 'massive';
    if (body.classList.contains('theme-bics')) return 'bics';

    return 'telesign';
  }

  private async _loadLogo(): Promise<void> {
    const logoName = this._getEffectiveLogoName();
    const cacheKey = logoName;

    if (MainLogo._svgCache.has(cacheKey)) {
      this._svgContent = MainLogo._svgCache.get(cacheKey)!;
      this._error = false;
      return;
    }

    this._loading = true;
    this._error = false;

    try {
      const svgPath = `./${logoName}-logo.svg`;
      const response = await globalThis.fetch(svgPath);

      if (!response.ok) {
        throw new Error(`Failed to load ${logoName} logo: ${response.status}`);
      }

      let svgContent = await response.text();

      svgContent = this._cleanSvg(svgContent);

      MainLogo._svgCache.set(cacheKey, svgContent);

      this._svgContent = svgContent;
      this._error = false;
    } catch (error) {
      globalThis.console?.error('Error loading logo:', error);
      this._error = true;
      this._svgContent = '';
    } finally {
      this._loading = false;
    }
  }

  private _cleanSvg(svgContent: string): string {
    let cleaned = svgContent.replace(/\s*(width|height)="[^"]*"/g, '');

    if (!cleaned.includes('viewBox')) {
      const widthMatch = svgContent.match(/width="([^"]*)"/);
      const heightMatch = svgContent.match(/height="([^"]*)"/);

      if (widthMatch && heightMatch) {
        const width = parseFloat(widthMatch[1]);
        const height = parseFloat(heightMatch[1]);
        if (!isNaN(width) && !isNaN(height)) {
          cleaned = cleaned.replace('<svg', `<svg viewBox="0 0 ${width} ${height}"`);
        }
      }
    }

    return cleaned;
  }

  private _getSizeStyles(): { [key: string]: string } {
    if (!this.size) {
      return {};
    }

    const size = this.size;

    if (/^\d+$/.test(size)) {
      return { width: `${size}px`, height: `${size}px` };
    }

    if (/^\d+(\.\d+)?(px|em|rem|%|vh|vw)$/.test(size)) {
      return { width: size, height: size };
    }

    const dimensionMatch = size.match(
      /^(\d+(?:\.\d+)?(?:px|em|rem|%|vh|vw)?)\s*[x×]\s*(\d+(?:\.\d+)?(?:px|em|rem|%|vh|vw)?)$/i
    );
    if (dimensionMatch) {
      const [, width, height] = dimensionMatch;
      return { width, height };
    }

    return { width: size, height: size };
  }

  private _getCurrentTheme(): string {
    const body = document.querySelector('body.sb-show-main');
    return body?.getAttribute('data-theme') || 'telesign';
  }

  private _observeThemeChanges(): void {
    const body = document.querySelector('body.sb-show-main');
    if (!body) return;

    const observer = new globalThis.MutationObserver(mutations => {
      mutations.forEach(mutation => {
        if (mutation.type === 'attributes' && mutation.attributeName === 'data-theme') {
          this._updateLogoBasedOnTheme();
        }
      });
    });

    observer.observe(body, {
      attributes: true,
      attributeFilter: ['data-theme'],
    });
  }

  private _updateLogoBasedOnTheme(): void {
    const currentTheme = this._getCurrentTheme();

    this.setAttribute('data-current-theme', currentTheme);

    if (currentTheme === 'massive') {
      this.name = 'massive';
      this.color = 'primary';
    } else if (currentTheme === 'bics') {
      this.name = 'bics';
      this.color = 'dark';
    } else {
      this.name = 'telesign';
      this.color = 'light';
    }

    this._loadLogo();
  }

  render(): TemplateResult {
    const logoName = this._getEffectiveLogoName();
    const sizeStyles = this._getSizeStyles();

    if (this._loading) {
      return html`
        <div class="logo-container" style=${styleMap(sizeStyles)}>
          <div class="loading">Loading...</div>
        </div>
      `;
    }

    if (this._error || !this._svgContent) {
      return html`
        <div class="logo-container" style=${styleMap(sizeStyles)}>
          <div class="error">Logo not found</div>
        </div>
      `;
    }

    const svgWithClass = this._svgContent.replace('<svg', `<svg class="${logoName}-logo"`);

    return html`
      <div class="logo-container" style=${styleMap(sizeStyles)}>${unsafeHTML(svgWithClass)}</div>
    `;
  }
}

declare global {
  interface HTMLElementTagNameMap {
    'main-logo': MainLogo;
  }
}

import React from 'react';
import { Meta, Title, Subtitle, Unstyled } from '@storybook/blocks';
import { capitalize, getVariableValue } from './utils';
import {
  PRIMARY_COLORS,
  ACCENT_COLORS,
  NEUTRAL,
  BASE,
  BLACK_WHITE,
  SUCCESS,
  WARNING,
  DANGER,
  INFORMATION,
  FOCUS,
  UI_COLORS,
  TEXT_COLORS,
  BACKGROUND_COLORS,
  ICON_COLORS,
  STROKE_COLORS,
  ALPHA_COLORS,
  EXTENDED_COLORS,
} from './utils';

<Meta title="Foundation/Colors" />

<div className="col-doc__wrapper">
  <div className="col-doc__container">
    <Unstyled>
      <Title>Colors</Title>
      <div className="col-intro-text">
        The color system defines a comprehensive set of tokens that ensure consistency, accessibility, and brand alignment across all products and platforms. It is organized into six primary categories — Primary, Accent, Neutral, Base, Semantic, and Extended — each with a distinct role in the visual language of the interface.
      </div>

      <Subtitle>Color Categories</Subtitle>

      <h3>Primary Colors</h3>
      <div>
        These are the most recognizable brand colors, used prominently across the UI. They serve as the backbone of the product's identity, applied to key actions like buttons, banners, links, and major navigation components. The primary palette reinforces brand recognition and should remain consistent across all touchpoints.
      </div>
      <ColorItem
        name="Primary"
        prefix="theme"
        values={PRIMARY_COLORS}
      />

      <h3>Accent Colors</h3>
      <div>
        Accent colors are used more selectively and strategically. Their purpose is to create visual contrast, highlight important information, and guide user attention without overwhelming the design. Accents should complement the primary palette and maintain brand harmony.
      </div>
      <ColorItem
        name="Accent"
        prefix="theme"
        values={ACCENT_COLORS}
      />

      <h3>Neutral Colors</h3>
      <div>
        The neutral palette consists of grayscale values—ranging from black and white to various shades of gray. These colors are foundational for building layouts, structuring hierarchy, and ensuring content legibility. Neutrals are also used for surfaces, dividers, containers, and backgrounds.
      </div>
      <ColorItem
        name="Neutral"
        prefix="theme"
        values={NEUTRAL}
      />
      <ColorItem
        name="Black & White"
        prefix="theme"
        values={BLACK_WHITE}
      />

      <h3>Base Colors</h3>
      <div>
        These are supporting colors used in UI components like input borders, tags, pills, and secondary surfaces. Base colors offer an extra level of customization, allowing brands to fine-tune component styling while preserving consistency and usability.
      </div>
      <ColorItem
        name="Base"
        prefix="theme"
        values={BASE}
      />

      <h3>Semantic Colors</h3>
      <div>
        Semantic tokens communicate system feedback and status. This includes color tokens for Success, Error, Warning, and Information states. Semantic colors play a critical role in accessibility and UX clarity by aligning with universally recognized signals for user feedback and interaction.
      </div>
      <ColorItem
        name="Success"
        prefix="theme"
        values={SUCCESS}
      />
      <ColorItem
        name="Warning"
        prefix="theme"
        values={WARNING}
      />
      <ColorItem
        name="Danger"
        prefix="theme"
        values={DANGER}
      />
      <ColorItem
        name="Information"
        prefix="theme"
        values={INFORMATION}
      />

      <h3>Focus Colors</h3>
      <div>
        Focus colors highlight interactive elements when they receive keyboard focus. This is crucial for
        accessibility and helps users navigate the interface using keyboard controls.
      </div>
      <ColorItem
        name="Focus"
        prefix="theme"
        values={FOCUS}
      />
      <h3>Extended Colors</h3>
      <div>
        The extended palette provides additional flexibility for unique UI needs, themes, or product variants. These colors are optional and used to expand the visual language when the primary, accent, or semantic tokens are not sufficient. Extended colors should be used sparingly and with intention to maintain harmony within the system.
      </div>
      <ColorItem
        name="Extended"
        prefix="colors-extended"
        values={EXTENDED_COLORS}
      />

      <Subtitle>Color Usage Classification</Subtitle>
      <div>
        Colors are further classified based on their functional application within the interface. This structure helps define clear and consistent usage across design and code.
      </div>
      <ColorItem
        name="UI Colors"
        description="Applied to interactive and visual interface elements such as buttons, toggles, badges, chips, and sliders. UI colors bring interactivity to life and should align with the hierarchy and role of the component (e.g., primary vs. secondary actions)."
        prefix="colors-ui"
        values={UI_COLORS}
      />
      <ColorItem
        name="Text Colors"
        description="Used for all typographic elements — including headers, body text, captions, labels, and links. Text colors should always maintain sufficient contrast against their background to meet accessibility standards. They typically derive from neutral, primary, or semantic categories depending on the context."
        prefix="colors-text"
        values={TEXT_COLORS}
      />
      <ColorItem
        name="Background Colors"
        description="Assigned to large surface areas including pages, cards, modals, drawers, and sections. These colors establish spatial zones and often derive from neutral, base, or extended palettes depending on the theme or product layer."
        prefix="colors-bg"
        values={BACKGROUND_COLORS}
      />
      <ColorItem
        name="Icon Colors"
        description="Used to colorize icons and symbolic figures. Icon colors should complement the surrounding text and UI elements while ensuring visibility and clarity. They often align with semantic or accent tones for status indicators or callouts."
        prefix="colors-icon"
        values={ICON_COLORS}
      />
      <ColorItem
        name="Stroke Colors"
        description="Used for outlines, borders, and dividers — supporting structure, visual grouping, and affordance. These colors often come from the base or neutral categories and must maintain enough contrast to distinguish components clearly."
        prefix="colors-stroke"
        values={STROKE_COLORS}
      />
      <ColorItem
        name="Alpha Colors"
        description="These are semi-transparent variations of existing tokens, primarily used for overlay states like hover, focus, disabled, or loading. Alpha colors allow for layering while preserving the visual tone of the underlying element. They are essential for creating subtle transitions and feedback in UI interactions."
        prefix="colors-alpha"
        values={ALPHA_COLORS}
      />
    </Unstyled>
  </div>
</div>

export const ColorItem = ({ name, description, values, prefix }) => {
  const [colorValues, setColorValues] = React.useState({});
  const [isLoaded, setIsLoaded] = React.useState(false);

  React.useEffect(() => {
    const timer = setTimeout(() => {
      const valueObj = {};
      values.forEach(colorName => {
        valueObj[colorName] = getVariableValue(colorName, prefix);
      });
      setColorValues(valueObj);
      setIsLoaded(true);
    }, 0);

    return () => clearTimeout(timer);
  }, [values, prefix]);

  return (
    <div className="color-item">
      <div className="color-item__title">{capitalize(name)}</div>
      {description && <div className="color-item__description">{description}</div>}
      <div className="color-item__container">
        {values.map((colorName) => {
          const isAlphaColor = prefix === 'colors-alpha';

          return (
            <div
              key={colorName}
              className="color-item__swatch-wrapper"
            >
              {isAlphaColor ? (
                <div className="color-item__swatch checkerboard">
                  <div
                    className="color-item__swatch-overlay"
                    style={{ backgroundColor: `var(--col-${prefix}-${colorName})` }}
                  ></div>
                </div>
              ) : (
                <div
                  className="color-item__swatch"
                  style={{ backgroundColor: `var(--col-${prefix}-${colorName})` }}
                ></div>
              )}
              <p className="color-item__variable-name">{`--col-${prefix}-${colorName}`}</p>
              <p className="color-item__color-value">
                {isLoaded ? colorValues[colorName] : 'Loading...'}
              </p>
            </div>
          );
        })}
      </div>
    </div>
  );
};

================================================
import React from 'react';
import { Meta, Title, Subtitle, Source } from '@storybook/blocks';
import { DEPTH } from './utils'

<Meta title="Foundation/Depth" />

<div className="col-doc__wrapper">
  <div className="col-doc__container">
    <Title>Depth</Title>
    <div className="col-intro-text">
      The depth system provides box-shadow tokens that create visual hierarchy and spatial relationships through elevation in the interface. These tokens allow components to appear raised from or recessed into the UI surface, adding dimension and focus while maintaining a cohesive visual language.
    </div>

    <DepthItem
      values={Object.keys(DEPTH['box-shadow'])}
    />
  </div>
</div>

export const DepthItem = ({ values }) => {
  const [depthValues, setDepthValues] = React.useState({});
  const [isLoaded, setIsLoaded] = React.useState(false);

  React.useEffect(() => {
    const timer = setTimeout(() => {
      const valueObj = {};
      values.forEach(depthName => {
        valueObj[depthName] = DEPTH['box-shadow'][depthName];
      });
      setDepthValues(valueObj);
      setIsLoaded(true);
    }, 0);

    return () => clearTimeout(timer);
  }, [values]);

  const isInverse = (name) => name.includes('inverse');

  return (
    <div className="token-item">
      <div className="token-item__container">
        {values.map((depthName) => (
          <div key={depthName} className="token-item__example">
            <div className="token-item__info-container">
              <div className="token-item__sample-wrapper">
                <div
                  className={`token-item__sample token-item__sample--depth ${isInverse(depthName) ? 'depth-inverse-sample' : ''}`}
                  style={{ boxShadow: depthValues[depthName] }}
                />
              </div>
              <div className="token-item__details">
                <div>{depthName}</div>
                <div className="token-item__value">{isLoaded ? depthValues[depthName] : 'Loading...'}</div>
                <div className="token-item__variable-name">--col-depth-box-shadow-{depthName}</div>
              </div>
            </div>
            <div className="token-item__code-container">
              <Source
                dark
                format="dedent"
                language="css"
                code={`box-shadow: var(--col-depth-box-shadow-${depthName});`}
              />
            </div>
          </div>
        ))}
      </div>
    </div>
  );
};

import React from 'react';
import { Meta, Title, Subtitle, Source, Unstyled } from '@storybook/blocks';
import GridMeta from '@/components/col-grid/col-grid.stories'
import { Callout, Container, DocsLinkTo } from '../components';
import {
  GRID_COLUMNS,
  GRID_ROWS,
  GRID_AREAS,
  GRID_AREA_ASSIGNMENTS,
  BREAKPOINTS,
  BREAKPOINT_VALUES,
  GRID_ROW_SPANS,
  GRID_COLUMN_SPANS,
  GRID_TOKEN_VALUES,
  LAYOUT_SIZES,
  getGridUtilityClass,
} from './utils';

<Meta title="Foundation/Grid" />

<div className="col-doc__wrapper">
  <div className="col-doc__container">
    <Unstyled>
      <Title>Grid</Title>
      <div className="col-intro-text">
        The grid system provides powerful layout utilities for creating responsive, flexible layouts using CSS Grid.
        It consists of container utilities for establishing grid contexts, column and row templates for structure,
        gap utilities for spacing, and item utilities for positioning and spanning. This comprehensive system enables
        designers and developers to build complex layouts with utility-first CSS classes while maintaining consistency
        across all platforms.
      </div>

      <Callout variant="info">
        **Looking for the component approach?** Check out the <DocsLinkTo title={GridMeta.title}>Grid Component</DocsLinkTo>
        for a more declarative way to build grid layouts with the same underlying utilities.
      </Callout>

      <Subtitle>Table of Contents</Subtitle>
      - [Grid Container Utilities](#grid-container-utilities)
        - [Display Classes](#display-classes)
        - [Grid Template Columns](#grid-template-columns)
        - [Grid Template Rows](#grid-template-rows)
      - [Gap Utilities](#gap-utilities)
        - [Universal Gap](#universal-gap)
        - [Column Gap](#column-gap)
        - [Row Gap](#row-gap)
      - [Grid Item Utilities](#grid-item-utilities)
        - [Column Spanning](#column-spanning)
        - [Row Spanning](#row-spanning)
      - [Responsive Utilities](#responsive-utilities)
        - [Responsive Columns](#responsive-columns)
        - [Responsive Spanning](#responsive-spanning)
      - [Advanced Grid Utilities](#advanced-grid-utilities)
        - [Auto-Fit Columns](#auto-fit-columns)
        - [Named Grid Areas](#named-grid-areas)

      # Grid Container Utilities
      <p>
        Grid container utilities establish a grid formatting context for child elements. These foundational classes
        convert any element into a grid container, enabling the use of all other grid utilities for layout control.
      </p>

      <div className="grid-section">
        ## Display Classes
        <p>
          The display classes create the basic grid container. Use <code>.col-grid</code> for block-level grid containers
          or <code>.col-inline-grid</code> for inline grid containers that flow with surrounding content.
        </p>

        <GridExample
          title="Block Grid Container"
          description="Creates a block-level grid container using .col-grid"
          gridClasses="col-grid col-grid-cols-3 col-grid-gap-m"
          cellCount={6}
          cellVariant="primary"
        />

        <GridExample
          title="Inline Grid Container"
          description="Creates an inline grid container using .col-inline-grid"
          gridClasses="col-inline-grid col-grid-cols-2 col-grid-gap-s"
          cellCount={4}
          cellVariant="secondary"
          cellSize="small"
        />

        <div className="usage-note">
          <h4>When to use</h4>
          <ul>
            <li><strong>.col-grid</strong> - Most common choice for layout containers, sections, and components</li>
            <li><strong>.col-inline-grid</strong> - When you need the grid to flow inline with other content</li>
          </ul>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div className="grid-section">
        ## Grid Template Columns
        <p>
          Column template utilities define the number and sizing of grid columns. The system provides utilities for
          1-12 columns using fractional units for equal distribution, plus a <code>none</code> value for resetting.
        </p>

        <GridExample
          title="Single Column"
          description="All items stack vertically in one column"
          gridClasses="col-grid col-grid-cols-1 col-grid-gap-m"
          cellCount={4}
          cellVariant="primary"
        />

        <GridExample
          title="Two Columns"
          description="Items distribute across two equal columns"
          gridClasses="col-grid col-grid-cols-2 col-grid-gap-m"
          cellCount={6}
          cellVariant="secondary"
        />

        <GridExample
          title="Three Columns"
          description="Items distribute across three equal columns"
          gridClasses="col-grid col-grid-cols-3 col-grid-gap-m"
          cellCount={9}
          cellVariant="accent"
        />

        <GridExample
          title="Four Columns"
          description="Items distribute across four equal columns"
          gridClasses="col-grid col-grid-cols-4 col-grid-gap-m"
          cellCount={8}
          cellVariant="default"
        />

        <GridExample
          title="Six Columns"
          description="Items distribute across six equal columns"
          gridClasses="col-grid col-grid-cols-6 col-grid-gap-s"
          cellCount={12}
          cellVariant="primary"
          cellSize="small"
        />

        <GridExample
          title="Twelve Columns"
          description="Traditional 12-column grid system"
          gridClasses="col-grid col-grid-cols-12 col-grid-gap-xs"
          cellCount={12}
          cellVariant="secondary"
          cellSize="small"
        />

        <div className="grid-token-reference">
          <h4>Available Column Classes</h4>
          <p>All column utilities follow the pattern <code>.col-grid-cols-[number]</code>:</p>
          <div className="grid-token-list">
            {GRID_COLUMNS.filter(col => col !== 'none').slice(0, 12).map(colCount => (
                <div key={colCount} className="grid-token-item">
                  .col-grid-cols-{colCount}
                </div>
              )
            )}
            <div className="grid-token-item">.col-grid-cols-none</div>
          </div>
          <p className="grid-token-note">
            <strong>CSS Variables:</strong> Each class maps to <code>--col-grid-columns-[number]</code> which resolves to
            <code>repeat([number], minmax(0, 1fr))</code> for equal column distribution.
          </p>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div className="grid-section">
        ## Grid Template Rows
        <p>
          Row template utilities define the number and sizing of grid rows. Similar to columns, these utilities
          provide explicit row control from 1-12 rows using fractional units for equal distribution.
        </p>

        <GridExample
          title="Two Rows"
          description="Items distribute across two equal rows"
          gridClasses="col-grid col-grid-cols-3 col-grid-rows-2 col-grid-gap-m"
          cellCount={6}
          cellVariant="primary"
        />

        <GridExample
          title="Three Rows"
          description="Items distribute across three equal rows with explicit height"
          gridClasses="col-grid col-grid-cols-2 col-grid-rows-3 col-grid-gap-m"
          cellCount={6}
          cellVariant="accent"
          cellSize="large"
        />

        <GridExample
          title="Row Reset"
          description="Using .col-grid-rows-none to reset row template"
          gridClasses="col-grid col-grid-cols-4 col-grid-rows-none col-grid-gap-m"
          cellCount={8}
          cellVariant="secondary"
        />

        <div className="grid-token-reference">
          <h4>Available Row Classes</h4>
          <p>All row utilities follow the pattern <code>.col-grid-rows-[number]</code>:</p>
          <div className="grid-token-list">
            {GRID_ROWS.filter(row => row !== 'none').map(rowCount => (
              <div key={rowCount} className="grid-token-item">
                .col-grid-rows-{rowCount}
              </div>
            ))}
            <div className="grid-token-item">.col-grid-rows-none</div>
          </div>
          <p className="grid-token-note">
            <strong>Note:</strong> Row templates are most useful when you need explicit control over row heights.
            In many cases, letting rows auto-size based on content is preferred.
          </p>
        </div>
      </div>

      # Gap Utilities
      <p>
        Gap utilities control the spacing between grid items using the design system's layout tokens.
        These utilities provide consistent spacing that adapts to your theme while offering fine-grained
        control over both universal gaps and directional spacing for columns and rows.
      </p>

      <div className="grid-section">
        ## Universal Gap
        <p>
          Universal gap utilities apply spacing between all grid items, both horizontally and vertically.
          These classes use the layout token system to ensure consistent spacing across your application.
        </p>

        <GridExample
          title="Extra Small Gap"
          description="Minimal spacing using .col-grid-gap-xs"
          gridClasses="col-grid col-grid-cols-3 col-grid-gap-xs"

        />

          cellCount={6}
          cellVariant="secondary"
        />

        <GridExample
          title="Medium Gap"
          description="Medium spacing using .col-grid-gap-m"
          gridClasses="col-grid col-grid-cols-3 col-grid-gap-m"
          cellCount={6}
          cellVariant="accent"
        />

        <GridExample
          title="Large Gap"
          description="Large spacing using .col-grid-gap-l"
          gridClasses="col-grid col-grid-cols-3 col-grid-gap-l"
          cellCount={6}
          cellVariant="default"
        />

        <GridExample
          title="Extra Large Gap"
          description="Extra large spacing using .col-grid-gap-xl"
          gridClasses="col-grid col-grid-cols-2 col-grid-gap-xl"
          cellCount={4}
          cellVariant="primary"
        />

        <div className="grid-token-reference">
          <h4>Available Gap Classes</h4>
          <p>All gap utilities follow the pattern <code>.col-grid-gap-[size]</code>:</p>
          <div className="grid-token-list">
            {LAYOUT_SIZES.map(size => (
              <div key={size} className="grid-token-item">.col-grid-gap-{size}</div>
            ))}
          </div>
          <p className="grid-token-note">
            <strong>CSS Variables:</strong> Each class maps to <code>--col-layout-spacing-[size]</code> tokens
            which provide consistent spacing values across the design system.
          </p>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div className="grid-section">
        ## Column Gap
        <p>
          Column gap utilities apply spacing only between columns (horizontally), leaving rows to touch vertically.
          This is useful for card grids, image galleries, or layouts where you want horizontal separation without vertical spacing.
        </p>

        <GridExample
          title="Column Gap Only - Small"
          description="Horizontal spacing only using .col-grid-gap-col-s"

            items to stack closely vertically but have clear horizontal separation.
          </p>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div className="grid-section">
        ## Row Gap
        <p>
          Row gap utilities apply spacing only between rows (vertically), leaving columns to touch horizontally.
          This is useful for stacked content, vertical lists, or layouts where you want vertical separation without horizontal spacing.
        </p>

        <GridExample
