      # Grid Item Utilities
      <p>
        Grid item utilities control how individual grid items span across columns and rows. These utilities
        enable flexible layouts where some items take up more space than others, creating dynamic and
        visually interesting grid compositions while maintaining alignment and structure.
      </p>

      <div className="grid-section">
        ## Column Spanning
        <p>
          Column spanning utilities allow grid items to stretch across multiple columns. Use these classes
          to create featured content, wide headers, or any layout where certain items need more horizontal space.
        </p>

        <GridExample
          title="Basic Column Spanning"
          description="Items spanning 2 and 3 columns in a 4-column grid"
          gridClasses="col-grid col-grid-cols-4 col-grid-gap-m"
          cellCount={0}
          showCode={false}
        >
          <GridCell variant="primary" className="col-span-2">Span 2</GridCell>
          <GridCell variant="secondary">1</GridCell>
          <GridCell variant="secondary">2</GridCell>
          <GridCell variant="accent" className="col-span-3">Span 3</GridCell>
          <GridCell variant="secondary">3</GridCell>
        </GridExample>


        <div className="grid-token-reference">
          <h4>Available Column Span Classes</h4>
          <p>All column span utilities follow the pattern <code>.col-span-[number]</code>:</p>
          <div className="grid-token-list">
            {GRID_COLUMN_SPANS.map(span => (
              <div key={span} className="grid-token-item">.col-span-{span}</div>
            ))}
            <div className="grid-token-item">.col-span-full</div>
          </div>
          <p className="grid-token-note">
            <strong>CSS Variables:</strong> Each class maps to <code>span [number]</code> which tells the item
            to span across that many columns. <code>.col-span-full</code> spans across all available columns.
          </p>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div className="grid-section">
        ## Row Spanning

      # Responsive Utilities
      <p>
        Responsive grid utilities enable mobile-first responsive design by applying different grid behaviors
        at specific breakpoints. These utilities follow the same patterns as other grid classes but include
        responsive prefixes to control when they take effect, allowing for adaptive layouts across devices.
      </p>

      <div className="grid-section">
        ## Responsive Columns
        <p>
          Responsive column utilities allow you to define different column layouts at different screen sizes.
          Start with a mobile-first approach and progressively enhance the layout for larger screens using
          breakpoint prefixes.
        </p>

        <GridExample
          title="Mobile-First Responsive Grid"
          description="1 column on mobile, 2 on small screens, 3 on medium, 4 on large"
          gridClasses="col-grid col-grid-cols-1 col-sm:grid-cols-2 col-md:grid-cols-3 col-lg:grid-cols-4 col-grid-gap-m"
          cellCount={8}
          cellVariant="primary"
        />

        <GridExample
          title="Simple Responsive Layout"
          description="Stack on mobile (1 col), side-by-side on tablet+ (2 cols)"
          gridClasses="col-grid col-grid-cols-1 col-md:grid-cols-2 col-grid-gap-l"
          cellCount={4}
          cellVariant="secondary"
        />

        <div className="grid-token-reference">
          <h4>Available Responsive Prefixes</h4>
          <p>All grid utilities can be prefixed with breakpoint modifiers:</p>
          <div className="grid-token-list">
            {BREAKPOINTS.map(bp => (
              <div key={bp} className="grid-token-item">col-{bp}: ({BREAKPOINT_VALUES[bp]}+)</div>
            ))}
          </div>
          <p className="grid-token-note">
            <strong>Mobile-First:</strong> Use base classes for mobile, then add responsive modifiers.
            For example: <code>.col-grid-cols-1 .col-md:grid-cols-2 .col-lg:grid-cols-4</code>
          </p>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div className="grid-section">
        ## Responsive Spanning
        <p>
          Responsive spanning utilities allow grid items to span different amounts of columns or rows
          at different breakpoints. This enables sophisticated layouts where content reflows and resizes
          based on available screen space.
        </p>

        <GridExample
          title="Responsive Column Spanning"
          description="Item 1 spans 2 columns on lg"
          gridClasses="col-grid col-grid-cols-1 col-md:grid-cols-2 col-lg:grid-cols-3 col-grid-gap-m"
          cellCount={0}
          showCode={false}
        >
          <GridCell variant="primary" className="col-span-full col-md:col-span-1 col-lg:col-span-1">
            Featured Content (full width)
          </GridCell>
          <GridCell variant="secondary" className='col-lg:span-2'>Item 1</GridCell>
          <GridCell variant="secondary">Item 2</GridCell>
        </GridExample>

        <div className="grid-token-reference">
          <h4>Responsive Spanning Patterns</h4>
          <p>Combine base spanning with responsive modifiers for adaptive layouts:</p>
          <div className="grid-token-list">
            <div className="grid-token-item">.col-span-full .col-md:span-2</div>
            <div className="grid-token-item">.col-span-1 .col-lg:span-3</div>
            <div className="grid-token-item">.row-span-1 .col-md:row-span-2</div>
            <div className="grid-token-item">.col-span-2 .col-xl:span-4</div>
          </div>
          <p className="grid-token-note">
            <strong>Best Practice:</strong> Start with the most constrained layout (mobile) and progressively
            enhance with more complex spanning for larger screens. This ensures content remains accessible
            and readable across all devices.
          </p>
        </div>
      </div>

      # Advanced Grid Utilities
      <p>
        Advanced grid utilities provide sophisticated layout patterns using modern CSS Grid features.
        These utilities enable dynamic column sizing with auto-fit and auto-fill behaviors, flexible
        sizing with minmax functions, and semantic layouts with named grid areas for complex interface designs.
      </p>

      <div className="grid-section">
        ## Auto-Fit Columns
        <p>
          Auto-fit utilities create responsive column layouts that automatically adjust the number of columns
          based on available space and minimum column width. These utilities use CSS Grid's auto-fit behavior
          with minmax sizing to create fluid, responsive layouts without media queries.
        </p>

        <GridExample
          title="Auto-Fit Medium (200px minimum)"
          description="Columns automatically fit with 200px minimum width"
          gridClasses="col-grid col-grid-cols-auto-fit-m col-grid-gap-m"
          cellCount={6}
          cellVariant="primary"
        />

        <GridExample
          title="Auto-Fit Small (160px minimum)"
          description="More columns with 160px minimum width"
          gridClasses="col-grid col-grid-cols-auto-fit-s col-grid-gap-m"
          cellCount={8}
          cellVariant="secondary"
        />

        <GridExample
          title="Auto-Fit Large (250px minimum)"
          description="Fewer, wider columns with 250px minimum width"
          gridClasses="col-grid col-grid-cols-auto-fit-l col-grid-gap-l"
          cellCount={5}
          cellVariant="accent"
        />

        <GridExample
          title="Auto-Fit Extra Large (300px minimum)"
          description="Wide columns with 300px minimum width"
          gridClasses="col-grid col-grid-cols-auto-fit-xl col-grid-gap-l"
          cellCount={4}
          cellVariant="default"
        />

        <div className="grid-token-reference">
          <h4>Available Auto-Fit Classes</h4>
          <p>Auto-fit utilities follow the pattern <code>.col-grid-cols-auto-fit-[size]</code>:</p>
          <div className="grid-token-list">
            <div className="grid-token-item">.col-grid-cols-auto-fit-0</div>
            <div className="grid-token-item">.col-grid-cols-auto-fit-xs</div>
            <div className="grid-token-item">.col-grid-cols-auto-fit-s</div>
            <div className="grid-token-item">.col-grid-cols-auto-fit-m</div>
            <div className="grid-token-item">.col-grid-cols-auto-fit-l</div>
            <div className="grid-token-item">.col-grid-cols-auto-fit-xl</div>
            <div className="grid-token-item">.col-grid-cols-auto-fit-2xl</div>
            <div className="grid-token-item">.col-grid-cols-auto-fit-3xl</div>
          </div>
          <p className="grid-token-note">
            <strong>CSS Variables:</strong> Each class maps to <code>--col-grid-autofit-[size]</code> which resolves to
            <code>repeat(auto-fit, minmax([size]px, 1fr))</code> for responsive column layouts.
          </p>
          <p className="grid-token-note">
            <strong>Use Cases:</strong> Perfect for card grids, image galleries, product catalogs, or any layout
            where you want items to automatically wrap and resize based on screen width without writing media queries.
          </p>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div className="grid-section">
        ## Named Grid Areas
        <p>
          Named grid area utilities enable semantic, template-based layouts using descriptive area names.
          This approach allows you to define layout templates with meaningful names like "header", "sidebar",
          and "content", making complex layouts more readable and maintainable.
        </p>

        <GridExample
          title="Dashboard Simple Layout"
          description="Two-column dashboard with navigation and main content"
          gridClasses="col-grid col-grid-areas-dashboard-simple col-grid-gap-m"
          cellCount={0}
          showCode={false}
        >
          <GridCell variant="secondary" className="col-grid-area-nav" size="medium">Navigation</GridCell>
          <GridCell variant="primary" className="col-grid-area-header" size="small">Header</GridCell>
          <GridCell variant="accent" className="col-grid-area-main" size="large">Main Content</GridCell>
        </GridExample>

        <GridExample
          title="Dashboard Complex Layout"
          description="Three-column dashboard with navigation, main, aside, and footer"
          gridClasses="col-grid col-grid-areas-dashboard-complex col-grid-gap-m"
          cellCount={0}
          showCode={false}
        >
          <GridCell variant="secondary" className="col-grid-area-nav" size="large">Navigation</GridCell>
          <GridCell variant="primary" className="col-grid-area-header" size="small">Header</GridCell>
          <GridCell variant="accent" className="col-grid-area-main" size="large">Main Content</GridCell>
          <GridCell variant="default" className="col-grid-area-aside" size="medium">Aside</GridCell>
          <GridCell variant="primary" className="col-grid-area-footer" size="small">Footer</GridCell>
        </GridExample>

        <GridExample
          title="Article Layout"
          description="Blog/article layout with header, content, sidebar, and footer"
          gridClasses="col-grid col-grid-areas-article-layout col-grid-gap-m"
          cellCount={0}
          showCode={false}
        >
          <GridCell variant="primary" className="col-grid-area-header" size="medium">Article Header</GridCell>
          <GridCell variant="accent" className="col-grid-area-content" size="large">Article Content</GridCell>
          <GridCell variant="secondary" className="col-grid-area-sidebar" size="medium">Sidebar</GridCell>
          <GridCell variant="default" className="col-grid-area-footer" size="small">Footer</GridCell>
        </GridExample>

        <div className="grid-token-reference">
          <h4>Available Grid Area Templates</h4>
          <p>Grid area templates define common layout patterns:</p>
          <div className="grid-token-list">
            {GRID_AREAS.map(area => (
              <div key={area} className="grid-token-item">.col-grid-areas-{area}</div>
            ))}
          </div>

          <h4>Available Grid Area Assignments</h4>
          <p>Individual area assignments for template positioning:</p>
          <div className="grid-token-list">
            {GRID_AREA_ASSIGNMENTS.map(area => (
              <div key={area} className="grid-token-item">.col-grid-area-{area}</div>
            ))}
          </div>

          <p className="grid-token-note">
            <strong>CSS Variables:</strong> Area templates map to <code>--col-grid-areas-[template]</code> which define
            the grid structure, while area assignments map to <code>--col-grid-area-[name]</code> for positioning elements.
          </p>
          <p className="grid-token-note">
            <strong>Semantic Layouts:</strong> Named grid areas make layouts self-documenting and easier to maintain.
            They're particularly valuable for complex interfaces where the relationship between layout areas
            needs to be clear and flexible.
          </p>
        </div>
      </div>
    </Unstyled>
  </div>
</div>

export const GridCell = ({ children, variant = 'default', size = 'medium', className = '' }) => {
  const cellClass = `grid-cell grid-cell--${variant} grid-cell--${size} ${className}`.trim();

  return (
    <div className={cellClass}>
      {children}
    </div>
  );
};

export const GridExample = ({
  title,
  description,
  gridClasses,
  children,
  cellCount = 6,
  cellVariant = 'default',
  cellSize = 'medium',
  showCode = true,
  className = ''
}) => {
  const defaultCells = React.useMemo(() => {
    return Array.from({ length: cellCount }, (_, index) => (
      <GridCell key={index} variant={cellVariant} size={cellSize}>
        {index + 1}
      </GridCell>
    ));
  }, [cellCount, cellVariant, cellSize]);

  const gridContent = children || defaultCells;

  return (
    <Container
      title={title}
      description={description}
      variant="example"
      className={className}
    >
      <div className={`grid-example-container ${gridClasses}`}>
        {gridContent}
      </div>
      {showCode && (
        <div className="grid-example-code">
          <code>
            {gridClasses.split(' ').map(cls => `.${cls}`).join(' ')}
          </code>
        </div>
      )}
    </Container>
  );
};



================================================
FILE: src/_storybook/foundation/radius.mdx
================================================
import React from 'react';
import { Meta, Title, Subtitle, Source } from '@storybook/blocks';
import { RADIUS } from './utils'

<Meta title="Foundation/Radius" />

<div className="col-doc__wrapper">
  <div className="col-doc__container">
    <Title>Radius</Title>
    <div className="col-intro-text">
      The border radius system provides a consistent approach to creating rounded corners across the interface. With options ranging from sharp corners to fully rounded shapes, these tokens help maintain visual harmony while allowing for appropriate distinction between UI elements.
    </div>

    <RadiusItem
      values={Object.keys(RADIUS)}
    />
  </div>
</div>

export const RadiusItem = ({ values }) => {
  const [radiusValues, setRadiusValues] = React.useState({});
  const [isLoaded, setIsLoaded] = React.useState(false);

  React.useEffect(() => {
    const timer = setTimeout(() => {
      const valueObj = {};
      values.forEach(radiusName => {
        valueObj[radiusName] = RADIUS[radiusName];
      });
      setRadiusValues(valueObj);
      setIsLoaded(true);
    }, 0);

    return () => clearTimeout(timer);
  }, [values]);

  return (
    <div className="token-item">
      <div className="token-item__container">
        {values.map((radiusName) => (
          <div key={radiusName} className="token-item__example">
            <div className="token-item__info-container">
              <div className="token-item__sample-wrapper">
                <div
                  className="token-item__sample token-item__sample--radius"
                  style={{ borderRadius: RADIUS[radiusName] }}
                />
              </div>
              <div className="token-item__details">
                <div>{radiusName}</div>
                <div className="token-item__value">{isLoaded ? radiusValues[radiusName] : 'Loading...'}</div>
                <div className="token-item__variable-name">--col-radius-size-{radiusName}</div>
              </div>
            </div>
            <div className="token-item__code-container">
              <Source
                dark
                format="dedent"
                language="css"
                code={`border-radius: var(--col-radius-size-${radiusName});`}
              />
            </div>
          </div>
        ))}
      </div>
    </div>
  );
};



================================================
FILE: src/_storybook/foundation/spacing.mdx
================================================
import React from 'react';
import { Meta, Title, Subtitle } from '@storybook/blocks';
import { SPACING, LAYOUT } from './utils';

<Meta title="Foundation/Spacing" />

<div className="col-doc__wrapper">
  <div className="col-doc__container">
    <Title>Spacing</Title>
    <div className="col-intro-text">
      The spacing system provides consistent measurements for layout and component design. It includes both a detailed component-level scale for precise UI element spacing and a broader design scale for overall layout composition.
    </div>

    <Subtitle>Spacing Scale (Component)</Subtitle>
    <p>
      Use the spacing scale when building individual components. It includes small increments necessary to create appropriate spatial relationships for detailed designs.
    </p>

    <SpacingItem
      values={Object.keys(SPACING)}
      tokenData={SPACING}
      tokenPrefix="spacing"
    />

    <Subtitle>Design Scale</Subtitle>
    <p>
      The design scale is used to organize components and other parts of the user interface in a complete page layout. It features larger increments that help control the density of a design. Use smaller design tokens to create denser compositions and direct relationships. Use larger tokens to increase whitespace and separate sections.
    </p>

    <SpacingItem
      values={Object.keys(LAYOUT)}
      tokenData={LAYOUT}
      tokenPrefix="layout"
    />
  </div>
</div>

export const SpacingItem = ({ values, tokenData, tokenPrefix }) => {
  const [spacingValues, setSpacingValues] = React.useState({});
  const [isLoaded, setIsLoaded] = React.useState(false);

  React.useEffect(() => {
    const timer = setTimeout(() => {
      const valueObj = {};
      values.forEach(spacingName => {
        valueObj[spacingName] = tokenData[spacingName];
      });
      setSpacingValues(valueObj);
      setIsLoaded(true);
    }, 0);

    return () => clearTimeout(timer);
  }, [values, tokenData]);

  return (
    <div className="token-item">
      <div className="token-item__container token-item__container--spacing">
        {values.map((spacingName) => (
          <div key={spacingName} className="token-item__example token-item__example--spacing">
            <div className="token-item__info-container">
              <div className="token-item__sample-wrapper">
                <div
                  className="token-item__sample token-item__sample--spacing"
                  style={{ width: tokenData[spacingName], height: tokenData[spacingName] }}
                />
              </div>
              <div className="token-item__details">
                <div>{spacingName}</div>
                <div className="token-item__value">{isLoaded ? spacingValues[spacingName] : 'Loading...'}</div>
                <div className="token-item__variable-name">--col-{tokenPrefix}-size-{spacingName}</div>
              </div>
            </div>
          </div>
        ))}
      </div>
    </div>
  );
};

FILE: src/_storybook/foundation/themes.mdx

import React from 'react';
import { Meta, Title, Subtitle, Unstyled } from '@storybook/blocks';
import { themes } from '@telesign/colibri';
import { Callout, Card, Container, Component, DocsLinkTo } from '../components';
import { useStorybookTheme } from '../hooks/useStorybookTheme';

<Meta title="Foundation/Themes" />

<div className="col-doc__wrapper">
  <div className="col-doc__container">
    <Unstyled>
      <Title>Themes</Title>
      <div className="col-intro-text">
        The theme system provides a powerful and flexible way to customize the visual appearance of components across different contexts, brands, and user preferences. Built on a foundation of design tokens and CSS custom properties, themes enable consistent styling while maintaining the flexibility to adapt to diverse design requirements.
      </div>

      <Subtitle>Table of contents</Subtitle>
      - [Theme System Overview](#theme-system-overview)
        - [What are Themes?](#what-are-themes)
        - [How Themes Work](#how-themes-work)
        - [Interactive Theme Testing](#interactive-theme-testing)
        - [Available Themes](#available-themes)
        - [Theme Implementation Methods](#theme-implementation-methods)
        - [Getting Started](#getting-started)
      - [Data-Theme Implementation](#data-theme-implementation)
        - [Basic Usage](#basic-usage)
        - [CSS Custom Properties](#css-custom-properties)
        - [Theme Inheritance](#theme-inheritance)
      - [Nested Themes](#nested-themes)
        - [CSS Cascade Behavior](#css-cascade-behavior)
        - [Parent-Child Relationships](#parent-child-relationships)
        - [Best Practices](#best-practices)
      - [Dynamic Token System](#dynamic-token-system)
        - [Component Token Overrides](#component-token-overrides)
        - [TypeScript Integration](#typescript-integration)
        - [Tokens vs Inline Styles](#tokens-vs-inline-styles)
        - [Advanced Token Usage](#advanced-token-usage)
        - [Token Performance](#token-performance)
      - [Custom Theme Registration](#custom-theme-registration)

      ## Theme System Overview

      ### What are Themes?
      <div>
        Themes are collections of design token values that define the visual appearance of your interface. Each theme contains the same set of tokens but with different values, allowing you to create variations like light and dark modes, brand-specific styling, or accessibility-focused alternatives without changing your component code.
      </div>

      ### How Themes Work
      <div>
        The Colibri theme system operates on three key principles:
      </div>
      <ul>
        <li><strong>Design Tokens:</strong> Universal variables that define colors, spacing, typography, and other design properties</li>
        <li><strong>CSS Custom Properties:</strong> Browser-native variables that enable dynamic theme switching</li>
        <li><strong>Component Integration:</strong> Seamless theme application through data attributes and token overrides</li>
      </ul>

      ### Interactive Theme Testing
      <Callout variant="info" icon="💡">
        Use the theme switcher in Storybook's toolbar (🌐 icon) to see how themes affect components in real-time throughout this documentation.
      </Callout>

      ### Available Themes
      <div>
        Colibri currently provides {Object.keys(themes).length} built-in themes, each designed for different use cases and aesthetic preferences. The themes include main color categories such as <strong>primary</strong>, <strong>accent</strong>, <strong>neutral</strong>, <strong>success</strong>, <strong>danger</strong>, <strong>warning</strong>, and <strong>information</strong> colors. For a complete overview of all available colors and their usage, please refer to the <DocsLinkTo title='Foundation/Colors'>Colors</DocsLinkTo> section in the documentation.
      </div>

      <div className="theme-preview-container">
        {Object.keys(themes).map((themeName) => (
          <ThemePreview
            key={themeName}
            themeName={themeName}
            themeConfig={themes[themeName]}
            colors={['primary-base', 'accent-base', 'neutral-500', 'success-base', 'danger-base', 'warning-base', 'information-base', 'focus']}
          />
        ))}
      </div>

      ### Theme Implementation Methods
      <div>
        Colibri supports multiple approaches to theme implementation, each suited to different use cases:
      </div>

      <div className="implementation-methods">
        <Card title="Data Attribute Themes">
          Apply themes globally or to specific sections using <code>data-theme</code> attributes. Perfect for application-wide theme switching and user preferences.
        </Card>
        <Card title="Dynamic Token Overrides">
          Customize individual components using the <code>.tokens</code> property. Ideal for component-specific styling and fine-grained control.
        </Card>
        <Card title="Nested Theme Contexts">
          Create theme hierarchies where child elements inherit and override parent theme values. Useful for complex layouts with multiple theme zones.
        </Card>
      </div>

      ### Getting Started
      <div>
        Ready to implement themes in your project? The following sections will guide you through each implementation method, from basic theme switching to advanced customization techniques. Each approach is designed to work seamlessly with Colibri's component library while providing the flexibility you need for your specific use case.
      </div>

      ## Data-Theme Implementation

      ### Basic Usage
      <div>
        The most straightforward way to apply themes is using the <code>data-theme</code> attribute on parent containers. This approach leverages CSS cascade to automatically theme all child elements without requiring individual component configuration.
      </div>

      ```html
      <!-- Apply theme to entire application -->
      <div data-theme="telesign">
        <header>
          <h1>My Application</h1>
          <nav>Navigation items inherit theme</nav>
        </header>
        <main>
          <p>All content automatically themed</p>
        </main>
      </div>

      <!-- Apply theme to specific sections -->
      <div data-theme="massive">
        <section>
          <h2>This section uses massive theme</h2>
          <p>All child elements inherit the theme</p>
        </section>
      </div>
      ```

      <Callout variant="tip" icon="💡">
        The <code>data-theme</code> attribute can be applied to any HTML element, making it flexible for both global application theming and section-specific styling.
      </Callout>

      ### CSS Custom Properties
      <div>
        When you apply a <code>data-theme</code> attribute, Colibri automatically makes theme-specific CSS custom properties available to all child elements. These properties follow a consistent naming convention using the <code>--col-theme-*</code> prefix.
      </div>

      ```css
      /* Theme tokens are automatically available as CSS custom properties */
      :root [data-theme="telesign"] {
        --col-theme-primary-base: #011ac2;
        --col-theme-accent-base: #1f5bff;
        --col-theme-neutral-500: #8c8ca1;
        --col-theme-success-base: #00b733;
        --col-theme-danger-base: #df1b42;
        --col-theme-warning-base: #ffa244;
        --col-theme-information-base: #077392;
        --col-theme-focus: #e5e8f9;
        /* ... and many more tokens */
      }

      /* Use theme tokens in your custom CSS */
      .my-custom-component {
        background-color: var(--col-theme-primary-base);
        color: var(--col-theme-white);
        border: 1px solid var(--col-theme-accent-base);
      }
      ```

      ### Theme Inheritance
      <div>
        Child elements automatically inherit theme properties from their parent containers. This cascading behavior ensures consistent theming throughout your application without requiring explicit theme configuration on every element.
      </div>

      <div className="data-theme-inheritance-demo">
        <DataThemeInheritanceDemo />
      </div>

      <Callout variant="info" icon="📋">
        <strong>Key Benefits:</strong>
        <ul>
          <li>Automatic theme inheritance for all child elements</li>
          <li>No individual component configuration required</li>
          <li>CSS custom properties available for custom styling</li>
          <li>Consistent <code>--col-theme-*</code> naming convention</li>
          <li>Works with any HTML structure or framework</li>
        </ul>
      </Callout>

      ## Nested Themes

      ### CSS Cascade Behavior
      <div>
        When multiple <code>data-theme</code> attributes are nested, CSS cascade rules determine which theme properties apply to each element. Child themes override parent themes, allowing for sophisticated theming hierarchies within your application.
      </div>

      ```html
      <!-- Parent theme applies to entire application -->
      <div data-theme="telesign">
        <header>Header uses default theme</header>

        <!-- Child theme overrides parent for this section -->
        <main data-theme="massive">
          <h1>This content uses massive theme</h1>
          <p>Overrides the parent default theme</p>
        </main>

        <footer>Footer returns to default theme</footer>
      </div>
      ```

      ### Parent-Child Relationships
      <div>
        Nested themes create clear boundaries where child elements inherit from their nearest themed ancestor. This enables complex layouts with multiple theme zones while maintaining predictable styling behavior.
      </div>

      <div className="nested-themes-demo">
        <NestedThemesDemo />
      </div>

      ### Best Practices
      <Callout variant="warning" icon="⚠️">
        <strong>Performance Considerations:</strong>
        <ul>
          <li>Limit nesting depth to 2-3 levels to avoid CSS specificity conflicts</li>
          <li>Prefer component-level token overrides for fine-grained control</li>
          <li>Use nested themes for distinct application sections, not individual components</li>
        </ul>
      </Callout>

      <Callout variant="tip" icon="💡">
        <strong>When to Use Nested Themes:</strong>
        <ul>
          <li><strong>Application sections:</strong> Different themes for header, main, sidebar</li>
          <li><strong>Modal overlays:</strong> Contrasting themes for focus and accessibility</li>
          <li><strong>Brand variations:</strong> Partner or white-label sections within main app</li>
          <li><strong>User preferences:</strong> Allow users to theme specific areas independently</li>
        </ul>
      </Callout>

      ## Dynamic Token System

      ### Component Token Overrides
      <div>
        For fine-grained control over individual component styling, Colibri provides the <code>.tokens</code> property on <code>ThemedComponent</code> instances. This approach allows you to override specific design tokens at the component level while maintaining theme consistency for unspecified tokens.
      </div>

      <Callout variant="info" icon="🎯">
        <strong>Important:</strong> Themes are applied using <code>data-theme</code> attributes on parent containers, and individual components can override specific tokens using the <code>.tokens</code> property.
      </Callout>

      <div>
        When using HTML files, you can set tokens programmatically using the <code>.tokens</code> property after the DOM is ready.
      </div>

      <Callout variant="tip" icon="💡">
        When setting tokens, you must <strong>use the correct token path structure</strong>. For instance, to modify the <strong>primary</strong> color, use the path <code>colors.ui.primary</code>. The <code>--col-</code> prefix is automatically appended to token names. For a complete reference of available colors and their corresponding token paths, see the <DocsLinkTo title='Foundation/Colors'>Colors</DocsLinkTo> documentation. For other tokens like <strong>spacing</strong>, <strong>typography</strong>, and more, refer to their respective sections in the <strong>Foundation</strong> section of the documentation.
      </Callout>

      #### HTML Files

      ```html
      <!-- Add a button to the DOM and give it an id -->
      <col-button color="primary" id="custom-button">Custom Themed Button</col-button>

      <!-- Register the component and set tokens -->
      <script type="module">
        import { registerColibriComponents, ColButton } from '@telesign/colibri';

        registerColibriComponents([ColButton]);

        // Set tokens after DOM is ready
        window.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', () => {
          // Get the button element by id
          const button = document.getElementById('custom-button');
          // If the button element exists, set the tokens
          if (button) {
            // Set the tokens by following the appropriate token path
            button.tokens = {
              colors: {
                ui: {
                  primary: '#ff6b35',
                }
            };
          }
        });
      </script>
      ```

      #### JavaScript Files

      ```javascript
      import { registerColibriComponents, ColButton } from '@telesign/colibri';

      registerColibriComponents([ColButton]);

      // Define custom tokens
      const customTokens = {
        colors: {
          ui: {
            primary: '#ff6b35',
          }
        }
      };

      // Method 1: Query existing element (with DOM event listener)
      document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', () => {
        const existingButton = document.querySelector('col-button');
        if (existingButton) {
          existingButton.tokens = customTokens;
        }
      });

      // Method 2: Create new element dynamically (no event listener needed)
      const newButton = document.createElement('col-button');
      newButton.tokens = customTokens;
      newButton.textContent = 'Custom Themed Button';
      document.body.appendChild(newButton);

      // The component automatically generates CSS custom properties:
      // --col-colors-ui-primary: #ff6b35
      ```

      <Callout variant="info" icon="💡">
        Use the theme switcher in Storybook's toolbar (🌐 icon) to see how themes affect the <strong>Default Button</strong> component in real-time.
      </Callout>

      <div className="token-override-demo">
        <TokenOverrideDemo />
      </div>

      ### TypeScript Integration
      <div>
        With native global types support, TypeScript automatically recognizes all Colibri web components without requiring manual type casting. The global types plugin generates `HTMLElementTagNameMap` declarations, providing full type safety and IntelliSense support out of the box.
      </div>

      ```typescript
      import { COLORS, ColButton } from '@telesign/colibri';
      import { registerColibriComponents } from '@telesign/colibri';

      registerColibriComponents([ColButton]);

      // Method 1: Query existing element (with DOM event listener)
      document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', () => {
        const buttonElement = document.querySelector('col-button'); // TypeScript knows this is ColButton
        if (buttonElement) {
          buttonElement.tokens = {
            colors: {
              ui: {
                primary: '#ff6b35',
              },
            },
          };
          // Full autocomplete and type checking available
          buttonElement.color = COLORS.PRIMARY;
          buttonElement.disabled = false;
        }
      });

      // Method 2: Create new element dynamically (no event listener needed)
      const newButton = document.createElement('col-button'); // TypeScript knows this is ColButton
      newButton.tokens = {
        colors: {
          ui: {
            primary: '#ff6b35',
          },
        },
      };

      newButton.textContent = 'Type-safe themed button';
      // Enum provides autocomplete and validation
      newButton.color = COLORS.PRIMARY;
      document.body.appendChild(newButton);
      ```

      <Callout variant="tip" icon="💡">
        <strong>Native TypeScript Benefits:</strong>
        <ul>
          <li>Automatic type recognition for all Colibri components via `HTMLElementTagNameMap`</li>
          <li>No manual type casting required - types are inferred automatically</li>
          <li>Full IntelliSense support for component properties and methods</li>
          <li>Compile-time validation prevents invalid property values</li>
          <li>Generated global types stay in sync with component definitions</li>
        </ul>
      </Callout>

      ### Tokens vs Inline Styles
      <div>
        The <code>.tokens</code> property and inline styles are <strong>mutually exclusive</strong>. When tokens are applied to a component, they will override any conflicting inline styles. You should choose one approach or the other, not combine them.
      </div>

      #### Using tokens Property

      ```javascript
      import { registerColibriComponents, ColButton } from '@telesign/colibri';

      registerColibriComponents([ColButton]);

      // ✅ CORRECT: Use tokens to override the default primary color
      const tokenButton = document.createElement('col-button');
      tokenButton.tokens = {
        colors: {
          ui: {
            'primary-base': '#011ac2',
          }
        }
      };
      tokenButton.textContent = 'Token-styled Button';
      document.body.appendChild(tokenButton);
      ```

      <div className="token-style-demo-wrapper">
        <TokenDemo type="tokens" title="✅ Using tokens Property" description="Override the default primary color with a custom color." />
      </div>

      #### Using Inline Styles

      ```javascript
      import { registerColibriComponents, ColButton } from '@telesign/colibri';

      registerColibriComponents([ColButton]);

      // ✅ CORRECT: Use inline styles to override the default primary color
      const styledButton = document.createElement('col-button');
      styledButton.style.backgroundColor = '#e74c3c';
      document.body.appendChild(styledButton);
      ```

      <div className="token-style-demo-wrapper">
        <TokenDemo type="inline" title="✅ Using Inline Styles" description="Override the default primary color with a custom color." />
      </div>

      #### Mixing Both (Invalid)

      ```javascript
      import { registerColibriComponents, ColButton } from '@telesign/colibri';

      registerColibriComponents([ColButton]);

      // ❌ INCORRECT: Don't mix both approaches, it will cause unpredictable behavior
      const conflictingButton = document.createElement('col-button');
      conflictingButton.style.backgroundColor = '#e74c3c'; // This will be overridden
      conflictingButton.tokens = {
        colors: {
          ui: {
            primary: '#df1b42' // This will win and override the inline style
          }
        }
      };
      conflictingButton.textContent = 'Conflicting tokens + inline styles';
      document.body.appendChild(conflictingButton);
      ```

      <div className="token-style-demo-wrapper">
        <TokenDemo type="invalid" title="❌ Mixing Both (Invalid)" description="Mixing tokens and inline styles is not supported and will cause unpredictable behavior. The inline style will win and override the token." />
      </div>

      <Callout variant="warning" icon="⚠️">
        <strong>Decision Matrix: When to Use Each Approach</strong>
        <ul>
          <li><strong>Use <code>tokens</code> when:</strong> You want theme-aware styling, need design system consistency, or plan to switch themes dynamically</li>
          <li><strong>Use inline styles when:</strong> You need one-off styling, have specific pixel values, or are prototyping quickly</li>
          <li><strong>Use <code>data-theme</code> when:</strong> You want to apply themes globally or to sections with multiple components</li>
        </ul>
      </Callout>

      ### Advanced Token Usage
      <div>
        Token overrides support complex patterns including conditional theming, responsive tokens, and composite token values for sophisticated component customization.
      </div>

      #### Conditional Token Overrides

      ```javascript
      import { registerColibriComponents, ColButton } from '@telesign/colibri';

      registerColibriComponents([ColButton]);

      const getAdaptiveTokens = (isDisabled: boolean) => ({
        colors: {
          ui: {
            primary: isDisabled ? '#00b733' : '#b7b7c0',
          }
        }
      });

      // Apply conditional tokens
      const button = document.querySelector('col-button');
      if (button) {
        button.tokens = getAdaptiveTokens(false);

        // Toggle state on click
        button.addEventListener('click', () => {
          const isCurrentlyDisabled = button.disabled;
          button.disabled = !isCurrentlyDisabled;
          button.tokens = getAdaptiveTokens(!isCurrentlyDisabled);
        });
      }
      ```

      <div className="advanced-token-demo-wrapper">
        <TokenDemo type="conditional" title="Conditional Token Overrides" description="Tokens change based on component state: click to toggle between active and disabled colors." />
      </div>

      #### Responsive Token Values

      ```javascript
      import { registerColibriComponents, ColButton } from '@telesign/colibri';

      registerColibriComponents([ColButton]);

      // Responsive token values using CSS functions
      const responsiveTokens = {
        spacing: {
          padding: 'clamp(12px, 4vw, 24px)',
          margin: 'max(8px, 1vw)'
        },
        typography: {
          size: 'clamp(14px, 2.5vw, 18px)'
        }
      };

      // Apply to components
      const responsiveCard = document.createElement('col-card');
      responsiveCard.tokens = responsiveTokens;
      document.body.appendChild(responsiveCard);
      ```

      <div className="advanced-token-demo-wrapper">
        <TokenDemo type="responsive" title="Responsive Token Values" description="Resize your browser to see responsive token values in action using clamp() and max() functions." />
      </div>

      #### Complex Nested Token Structure

      ```javascript
      import { registerColibriComponents, ColButton } from '@telesign/colibri';

      registerColibriComponents([ColButton]);

      const complexTokens = {
        colors: {
          ui: {
            accent: '#1f5bff',
          }
        },
        depth: {
          'box-shadow': {
            l: '0 8px 32px rgba(31, 91, 255, 0.15)',
          }
        }
      };

      const complexButton = document.createElement('col-button');
      complexButton.tokens = complexTokens;
      document.body.appendChild(complexButton);
      ```

      <div className="advanced-token-demo-wrapper">
        <TokenDemo type="composite" title="Complex Nested Token Structure" description="Multiple tokens working together for sophisticated component styling with coordinated colors and spacing." />
      </div>

      ### Token Performance
      <div>
        Token overrides are highly performant because they leverage CSS custom properties, which are processed natively by the browser. The updated token flattening system uses the <code>--col-</code> prefix for optimal CSS generation and minimal runtime overhead.
      </div>

      <Callout variant="info" icon="⚡">
        <strong>Performance Benefits:</strong>
        <ul>
          <li><strong>Native CSS processing:</strong> Tokens become CSS custom properties, processed by the browser's CSS engine</li>
          <li><strong>Minimal JavaScript overhead:</strong> Token flattening happens once during component creation</li>
          <li><strong>Optimal prefix structure:</strong> <code>--col-</code> prefix reduces CSS size and improves parsing speed</li>
          <li><strong>Dynamic updates:</strong> Changing token values updates all dependent styles automatically</li>
        </ul>
      </Callout>

      ```css
      /* Generated CSS custom properties with optimized --col- prefix */
      .themed-component {
        --col-colors-primary: #007bff;
        --col-colors-brand-primary: #ff6b35;
        --col-spacing-medium: 16px;
        --col-spacing-padding: clamp(12px, 4vw, 24px);

        /* Component styles automatically use these tokens */
        background-color: var(--col-colors-primary);
        padding: var(--col-spacing-medium);
      }
      ```

      <Callout variant="tip" icon="🚀">
        <strong>Best Practices for Performance:</strong>
        <ul>
          <li>Prefer token overrides over frequent inline style changes for better browser optimization</li>
          <li>Use semantic token names that can be shared across multiple components</li>
          <li>Leverage CSS custom property inheritance for efficient theme propagation</li>
          <li>Test theme switching performance with large numbers of themed components</li>
        </ul>
      </Callout>

      ## Custom Theme Registration
      <div>
        For runtime theme creation, Colibri provides the <code>registerTheme()</code> function to dynamically register custom themes that can be used with <code>data-theme</code> attributes.
      </div>

      ```javascript
      import { registerTheme } from '@telesign/colibri';

      // Register a custom neon theme
      registerTheme('neon', {
        'primary-base': '#ff00ff',
        'primary-dark': '#cc00cc',
        'accent-base': '#00ffff',
        'accent-dark': '#00cccc',
        'neutral-900': '#1a1a1a',
        'white': '#ffffff'
      });

      // Apply the custom theme to a container
      const container = document.querySelector('.my-app');
      container.setAttribute('data-theme', 'neon');
      ```

      <div className="custom-theme-demo-wrapper">
        <CustomThemeDemo />
      </div>

      <Callout variant="tip" icon="🎨">
        <strong>Custom Theme Best Practices:</strong>
        <ul>
          <li>Only override the tokens you need - unspecified tokens will fall back to defaults</li>
          <li>Use semantic theme names that describe the purpose (e.g., 'dark', 'high-contrast')</li>
          <li>Test custom themes across all components to ensure consistency</li>
          <li>Consider accessibility when choosing color combinations</li>
        </ul>
      </Callout>
    </Unstyled>

  </div>
</div>

export const DataThemeInheritanceDemo = () => {
  return (
    <div className="inheritance-demo-container">
      {Object.keys(themes).map(themeName => (
        <div key={themeName} className="inheritance-demo-section" data-theme={themeName}>
          <div className="inheritance-demo-header">
            <h4>{themeName.charAt(0).toUpperCase() + themeName.slice(1)} Theme Container</h4>
            <code>data-theme="{themeName}"</code>
          </div>
          <div className="inheritance-demo-content">
            <div className="inheritance-demo-card">
              <h5>Child Card</h5>
              <p>This card inherits the {themeName} theme automatically</p>
              <button className="inheritance-demo-button">Themed Button</button>
            </div>
            <div className="inheritance-demo-list">
              <h5>Child List</h5>
              <ul>
                <li>List item inherits {themeName} theme</li>
                <li>No explicit theming needed</li>
                <li>Automatic theme adaptation</li>
              </ul>
            </div>
          </div>
        </div>
      ))}
    </div>
  );
};

export const ThemePreview = ({ themeName, themeConfig, colors }) => {
  return (
    <div
      className="theme-preview-item"
      style={{
        border: `1px solid ${themeConfig['accent-base']}`,
        backgroundColor: themeConfig['white'],
      }}
    >
      <div className="theme-preview-header">
        <h4
          style={{
            color: themeConfig['accent-base'],
          }}
        >
          {themeName.charAt(0).toUpperCase() + themeName.slice(1)} Theme
        </h4>
        <div
          className="theme-preview-badge"
          style={{
            backgroundColor: themeConfig['base-light'],
            color: themeConfig['base-alt'],
          }}
        >
          {themeName}
        </div>
      </div>
      <div className="theme-preview-colors-table">
        {colors.map((color, index) => (
          <div
            key={`${themeName}-${color}-${index}`}
            className="theme-color-row"
            style={{
              backgroundColor: themeConfig['neutral-50'],
            }}
          >
            <div
              className="theme-color-swatch-circle"
              style={{
                backgroundColor: themeConfig[color],
                color: themeConfig['base-dark'],
              }}
            />
            <div
              className="theme-color-token-name"
              style={{
                color: themeConfig['primary-base'],
              }}
            >
              --col-theme-{color}
            </div>
            <div
              className="theme-color-hex-value"
              style={{
                backgroundColor: themeConfig['base-light'],
                color: themeConfig['base-alt'],
              }}
            >
              {themeConfig[color]}
            </div>
          </div>
        ))}
      </div>
    </div>
  );
};

export const NestedThemesDemo = () => {
  return (
    <div className="nested-themes-container">
      {Object.keys(themes)
        .filter(parentTheme => parentTheme !== 'telesign')
        .map((parentTheme) => (
        <div key={parentTheme} className="nested-themes-example" data-theme={parentTheme}>
          <div className="nested-themes-parent">
            <div className="nested-themes-parent-header">
              <h4>Application Container</h4>
              <code>data-theme="{parentTheme}"</code>
            </div>
            <div className="nested-themes-parent-content">
              <p>This is the parent application using {parentTheme} theme.</p>

              {Object.keys(themes)
                .filter(childTheme => childTheme !== parentTheme)
                .slice(0, 1)
                .map((childTheme) => (
                <div key={childTheme} className="nested-themes-child" data-theme={childTheme}>
                  <div className="nested-themes-child-header">
                    <h5>Nested Section</h5>
                    <code>data-theme="{childTheme}"</code>
                  </div>
                  <div className="nested-themes-child-content">
                    <p>This nested section overrides with {childTheme} theme.</p>
                    <button className="nested-themes-button">Child Button</button>
                  </div>
                </div>
              ))}

              <div className="nested-themes-parent-footer">
                <p>Footer content returns to parent {parentTheme} theme.</p>
                <button className="nested-themes-button">Parent Button</button>
              </div>
            </div>
          </div>
        </div>
      ))}
    </div>

);
};

export const TokenOverrideDemo = () => {
  const currentTheme = useStorybookTheme();
  const themeConfig = themes[currentTheme];
  const primaryColor = themeConfig?.['primary-base'] || '#011ac2';

return (

<Container
  title="Before and After Token Override"
  variant="showcase"
  className="token-override-showcase"
>
  <div className="token-override-comparison">
    <div className="token-override-before">
      <h5>Before ({currentTheme.charAt(0).toUpperCase() + currentTheme.slice(1)} Theme)</h5>
      <Component text="Default Button" variant="default" />
      <p>Uses active theme tokens</p>
      <div className="token-override-tokens">
        <span className="token-override-token">--col-colors-ui-primary: {primaryColor}</span>
      </div>
    </div>
    <div className="token-override-after">
      <h5>After (Custom Tokens)</h5>
      <Component text="Custom Themed Button" variant="custom" />
      <p>Uses custom token overrides</p>
      <div className="token-override-tokens">
        <span className="token-override-token">--col-colors-ui-primary: #ff6b35</span>
      </div>
    </div>
  </div>
</Container>
); };

export const CustomThemeDemo = () => {
  const [isRegistered, setIsRegistered] = React.useState(false);

const registerNeonTheme = () => {
// Import registerTheme dynamically to avoid issues in Storybook
import('@telesign/colibri').then(({ registerTheme }) => {
registerTheme('neon', {
'primary-base': '#ff00ff',
'primary-dark': '#cc00cc',
'accent-base': '#00ffff',
'accent-dark': '#00cccc',
'neutral-900': '#1a1a1a',
'white': '#ffffff'
});
setIsRegistered(true);
});
};

const applyNeonTheme = () => {
const Container = document.querySelector('.custom-theme-demo-container');
if (Container) {
Container.setAttribute('data-theme', 'neon');
}
};

const resetTheme = () => {
const Container = document.querySelector('.custom-theme-demo-container');
if (Container) {
Container.setAttribute('data-theme', 'telesign');
}
};

return (

<Container title="Custom Theme Registration Demo" className="custom-theme-demo-wrapper">
  <div className="custom-theme-demo-controls">
    <button
      onClick={registerNeonTheme}
      disabled={isRegistered}
      className="custom-theme-demo-button"
    >
      {isRegistered ? '✅ Neon Theme Registered' : 'Register Neon Theme'}
    </button>
    <button onClick={applyNeonTheme} disabled={!isRegistered} className="custom-theme-demo-button">
      Apply Neon Theme
    </button>
    <button onClick={resetTheme} className="custom-theme-demo-button">
      Reset to Default
    </button>
  </div>
  <div className="custom-theme-demo-container" data-theme="telesign">
    <Component text="Custom Themed Component" variant="default" />
    <p className="custom-theme-demo-description">
      This component will change appearance when the custom neon theme is applied.
    </p>
  </div>
</Container>
); };

export const TokenDemo = ({
  type,
  title,
  description,
  className = '',
}) => {
  const [isDisabled, setIsDisabled] = React.useState(false);

const getButtonText = () => {
switch (type) {
case 'tokens':
return 'Token-styled Button';
case 'inline':
return 'Inline-styled Button';
case 'invalid':
return 'Conflicting tokens + inline styles';
case 'conditional':
return isDisabled ? 'Disabled State' : 'Active State (Click to toggle)';
case 'responsive':
return 'Responsive padding, font-size, and border-radius using clamp() and max()';
case 'composite':
return 'Complex styling with multiple coordinated tokens';
default:
return 'Demo button';
}
};

const handleClick = () => {
if (type === 'conditional') {
setIsDisabled(!isDisabled);
}
};

const componentClassName = type === 'conditional' && isDisabled ? 'disabled' : '';
const isInteractive = type === 'conditional';

return (

<Container title={title} description={description} className={className}>
  <Component
    text={getButtonText()}
    variant={type}
    onClick={isInteractive ? handleClick : undefined}
    disabled={isDisabled}
    className={componentClassName}
  />
</Container>
); };



================================================
FILE: src/_storybook/foundation/typography.mdx
================================================
import { Meta, Title, Subtitle, Typeset, Source } from '@storybook/blocks';
import { TYPOGRAPHY } from './utils';

<Meta title="Foundation/Typography" />

export const theme = () => document.querySelector('body').getAttribute('data-theme');

export const baseSegoeUIFont = {
  name: 'Segoe UI',
  url: null,
  description: 'Primary font for the Colibri Design System, locally hosted',
  fallbacks: '"Inter", -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, "Helvetica Neue", Arial, sans-serif',
};

export const baseCourierFont = {
  name: 'Courier Monospace',
  url: null,
  description: 'Monospace font for code blocks and technical content',
  fallbacks: 'monospace',
};

export const fontFamilies = {
  telesign: {
    primary: baseSegoeUIFont,
    secondary: baseCourierFont,
  },
  massive: {
    primary: baseSegoeUIFont,
    secondary: baseCourierFont,
  },
  bics: {
    primary: baseSegoeUIFont,
    secondary: baseCourierFont,
  },
};

export const getCurrentFont = (type = 'primary') => {
  const currentTheme = theme() || 'telesign';
  const themeFamily = fontFamilies[currentTheme] || fontFamilies.telesign;
  return themeFamily[type] || themeFamily.primary;
};

export const SampleText = 'Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit.';

<div className="col-doc__wrapper">
  <div className="col-doc__container">
    <Title>Typography</Title>
    <div className="col-intro-text">
      The typography system defines the core text elements used throughout our product ecosystem. It consists of carefully calibrated font families, size scales, line heights, and weight variations that ensure optimal readability and clear information hierarchy. This comprehensive token system enables designers and developers to implement consistent typography that meets accessibility standards while maintaining brand fidelity across all platforms.
    </div>

    <Subtitle>Font Families</Subtitle>
    <div className="font-family-section">
      <h3>{getCurrentFont().name}</h3>
      <p>
      Colibri Design System uses <b>{getCurrentFont().name}</b> as its primary font family, a versatile and modern typeface that provides excellent readability across devices and screen sizes.
    </p>
      <div className="primary-font-preview">
        ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ<br />
        abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz<br />
        0123456789!@#$%^&*()
      </div>
    </div>

    <div className="font-family-section">
      <h3>Courier</h3>
      <p>
        Colibri Design System uses <b>{getCurrentFont('secondary').name}</b> as its monospace font family, optimized for code blocks, terminal output, and technical content.
      </p>
      <div className="secondary-font-preview">
        ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ<br />
        abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz<br />
        0123456789!@#$%^&*()
      </div>
    </div>

    <Subtitle>Typography Styles</Subtitle>
    <p>
      Colibri defines a set of typography styles that combine font size, weight, and line height to create a consistent type system across all interfaces. These styles are organized into three main categories: Heading, Body, and Supporting text.
    </p>

    <div className="typography-section">
      <h3>Heading</h3>
      <p>
        Heading styles are used for titles, subtitles, and section headers. They establish the information hierarchy and help users scan and navigate content. Headings should be used in descending order (H1 to H6) to maintain proper document structure.
      </p>

      <h4>H1</h4>
      <p>
        H1 headings are the most prominent text elements, typically used for page titles and major section headers. They use our largest font size (54px) with semilight weight to create strong visual hierarchy while maintaining an elegant, spacious feel. Use H1 sparingly - generally once per page - to establish the primary topic or purpose of the content.
      </p>

      <p>To set a H1 heading, you can use the native <code>h1</code> HTML tag:</p>

      <div className="typography-example">
        <div className="code-example">
          <Source dark format="dedent" language="html" code={`<h1>${SampleText}</h1>`} />
        </div>
        <p>or the <code>.h1</code> class:</p>
        <div className="code-example">
          <Source dark format="dedent" language="html" code={`<div class="h1">${SampleText}</div>`} />
        </div>
        <Typeset
          fontFamily={`"${getCurrentFont().name}", ${getCurrentFont().fallbacks}`}
          fontSizes={[parseInt(TYPOGRAPHY['7xl'])]}
          fontWeight={400}
          sampleText={SampleText}
        />

        <h4>H2</h4>
        <p>
          H2 headings are used for major section titles within a page, creating clear divisions between primary content areas. At 42px with semilight weight, they maintain strong visibility while being visually subordinate to H1 headings. Use H2s to break up long content into scannable sections and establish a logical content structure. Each major section of your page should typically have its own H2 heading.
        </p>

        <p>To set a H2 heading, you can use the native <code>h2</code> HTML tag:</p>

        <div className="code-example">
          <Source dark format="dedent" language="html" code={`<h2>${SampleText}</h2>`} />
        </div>
        <p>or the <code>.h2</code> class:</p>
        <div className="code-example">
          <Source dark format="dedent" language="html" code={`<div class="h2">${SampleText}</div>`} />
        </div>
        <Typeset
          fontFamily={`"${getCurrentFont().name}", ${getCurrentFont().fallbacks}`}
          fontSizes={[parseInt(TYPOGRAPHY['5xl'])]}
          fontWeight={400}
          sampleText={SampleText}
        />

        <h4>H3</h4>
        <p>
          H3 headings denote subsections within the larger sections established by H2 headings. At 32px with regular weight, they provide clear subsection differentiation while maintaining visual hierarchy. Use H3s to organize related content into more digestible chunks and create a more granular content structure. They're particularly effective for complex sections that contain multiple distinct topics.
        </p>

        <p>To set a H3 heading, you can use the native <code>h3</code> HTML tag:</p>

        <div className="code-example">
          <Source dark format="dedent" language="html" code={`<h3>${SampleText}</h3>`} />
        </div>
        <p>or the <code>.h3</code> class:</p>
        <div className="code-example">
          <Source dark format="dedent" language="html" code={`<div class="h3">${SampleText}</div>`} />
        </div>
        <Typeset
          fontFamily={`"${getCurrentFont().name}", ${getCurrentFont().fallbacks}`}
          fontSizes={[parseInt(TYPOGRAPHY['3xl'])]}
          fontWeight={400}
          sampleText={SampleText}
        />

        <h4>H4</h4>
        <p>
          H4 headings are used for minor section titles or prominent content points within subsections. At 28px with regular weight, they provide emphasis while integrating more naturally with surrounding content. These headings are useful for highlighting specific details, features, or topics within a subsection, making complex information more scannable and accessible.
        </p>

        <p>To set a H4 heading, you can use the native <code>h4</code> HTML tag:</p>

        <div className="code-example">
          <Source dark format="dedent" language="html" code={`<h4>${SampleText}</h4>`} />
        </div>
        <p>or the <code>.h4</code> class:</p>
        <div className="code-example">
          <Source dark format="dedent" language="html" code={`<div class="h4">${SampleText}</div>`} />
        </div>

        <Typeset
          fontFamily={`"${getCurrentFont().name}", ${getCurrentFont().fallbacks}`}
          fontSizes={[parseInt(TYPOGRAPHY['2xl'])]}
          fontWeight={400}
          sampleText={SampleText}
        />

        <h4>H5</h4>
        <p>
          H5 headings function as subtle dividers for very specific content segments or as emphasized introductory text. At 20px with regular weight, they integrate closely with body text while providing sufficient distinction. Use H5s for specialized content segments, lists of features, or to introduce important paragraphs that need slightly more prominence than standard body text.
        </p>

        <p>To set a H5 heading, you can use the native <code>h5</code> HTML tag:</p>

        <div className="code-example">
          <Source dark format="dedent" language="html" code={`<h5>${SampleText}</h5>`} />
        </div>
        <p>or the <code>.h5</code> class:</p>
        <div className="code-example">
          <Source dark format="dedent" language="html" code={`<div class="h5">${SampleText}</div>`} />
        </div>

        <Typeset
          fontFamily={`"${getCurrentFont().name}", ${getCurrentFont().fallbacks}`}
          fontSizes={[parseInt(TYPOGRAPHY.l)]}
          fontWeight={400}
          sampleText={SampleText}
        />

        <h4>H6</h4>
        <p>
          H6 headings are the smallest heading elements, used for minor subdivisions or emphasized inline content. At 16px (matching body text size) with semibold weight, they maintain heading status through weight distinction rather than size. Use H6s sparingly for small content labels, important notes within body text, or to create subtle structural divisions in dense information displays.
        </p>

        <p>To set a H6 heading, you can use the native <code>h6</code> HTML tag:</p>

        <div className="code-example">
          <Source dark format="dedent" language="html" code={`<h6>${SampleText}</h6>`} />
        </div>
        <p>or the <code>.h6</code> class:</p>
        <div className="code-example">
          <Source dark format="dedent" language="html" code={`<div class="h6">${SampleText}</div>`} />
        </div>

        <Typeset
          fontFamily={`"${getCurrentFont().name}", ${getCurrentFont().fallbacks}`}
          fontSizes={[parseInt(TYPOGRAPHY.m)]}
          fontWeight={600}
          sampleText={SampleText}
        />
      </div>
    </div>

    <div className="typography-section">
      <h3>Body</h3>
      <p>
        Body styles are used for the main content of a page. They are designed for optimal readability in different contexts, from paragraphs to lists, captions, and labels. The paragraph (<code>&lt;p&gt;</code>) element is the recommended semantic tag for body text, combined with our utility classes to apply the appropriate size.
      </p>

      <h4>Regular (L)</h4>
      <p>
        Regular L (20px) body text is used for primary content that requires enhanced readability or emphasis, such as introductory paragraphs, important statements, or content aimed at older audiences. The larger size creates a more comfortable reading experience for longer text blocks while maintaining proper information hierarchy when paired with headings.
      </p>

      <p>To set a regular large paragraph, you can use the <code>.text-l</code> class:</p>

      <div className="code-example">
        <Source dark format="dedent" language="html" code={`<p class="text-l">${SampleText}</p>`} />
      </div>
      <Typeset
        fontFamily={`"${getCurrentFont().name}", ${getCurrentFont().fallbacks}`}
        fontSizes={[parseInt(TYPOGRAPHY.l)]}
        fontWeight={400}
        sampleText={SampleText}
      />

      <h4>Regular (M)</h4>
      <p>
        Regular M (16px) body text is our standard size for most paragraph content, offering optimal readability across devices and screen sizes. This versatile size works well for general content, articles, descriptions, and most interface text. It provides a balanced reading experience that works well in both dense layouts and more spacious designs.
      </p>

      <p>To set a regular medium paragraph, you can use the <code>.text-m</code> class:</p>

      <div className="code-example">
        <Source dark format="dedent" language="html" code={`<p class="text-m">${SampleText}</p>`} />
      </div>
      <Typeset
        fontFamily={`"${getCurrentFont().name}", ${getCurrentFont().fallbacks}`}
        fontSizes={[parseInt(TYPOGRAPHY.m)]}
        fontWeight={400}
        sampleText={SampleText}
      />

      <h4>Regular (S)</h4>
      <p>
        Regular S (14px) body text is used for secondary content, compact UI elements, or supporting information where space is limited but readability remains important. This size works well for sidebar content, secondary descriptions, metadata, and interfaces with higher information density requirements.
      </p>

      <p>To set a regular small paragraph, you can use the <code>.text-s</code> class:</p>

      <div className="code-example">
        <Source dark format="dedent" language="html" code={`<p class="text-s">${SampleText}</p>`} />
      </div>
      <Typeset
        fontFamily={`"${getCurrentFont().name}", ${getCurrentFont().fallbacks}`}
        fontSizes={[parseInt(TYPOGRAPHY.s)]}
        fontWeight={400}
        sampleText={SampleText}
      />

      <h4>Regular (XS)</h4>
      <p>
        Regular XS (12px) body text is reserved for tertiary content, fine print, legal information, or highly compact UI elements. Use this size sparingly as it approaches minimum readability thresholds, particularly for users with visual impairments. Ensure sufficient contrast when implementing this text size.
      </p>

      <p>To set a regular extra small paragraph, you can use the <code>.text-xs</code> class:</p>

      <div className="code-example">
        <Source dark format="dedent" language="html" code={`<p class="text-xs">${SampleText}</p>`} />
      </div>

        fontFamily={`"${getCurrentFont().name}", ${getCurrentFont().fallbacks}`}
        fontSizes={[parseInt(TYPOGRAPHY.xs)]}
        fontWeight={400}
        sampleText={SampleText}
      />
    </div>

    <div className="typography-section">
      <h3>Supporting</h3>
      <p>
        Supporting text styles are used for auxiliary content such as helper text, labels, code snippets, and underlined text. These styles provide additional context or emphasis while maintaining clear visual hierarchy through size, weight, and decoration variations.
      </p>

      <h4>Helper (S)</h4>
      <p>
        Helper S (14px) provides contextual guidance and additional information with optimal readability. Typically used for form field instructions, tooltips, or explanatory notes where space allows for comfortable reading. The regular weight and appropriate line height ensure clear communication while maintaining visual subordination to primary content.
      </p>

      <p>To set helper text in size S, you can use the <code>.helper-s</code> class:</p>

      <div className="code-example">
        <Source dark format="dedent" language="html" code={`<span class="helper-s">${SampleText}</span>`} />
      </div>
      <Typeset
        fontFamily={`"${getCurrentFont().name}", ${getCurrentFont().fallbacks}`}
        fontSizes={[parseInt(TYPOGRAPHY.s)]}
        fontWeight={400}
        sampleText={SampleText}
      />

      <h4>Helper (XS)</h4>
      <p>
        Helper XS (12px) offers compact contextual information where space is limited. Ideal for inline hints, small form fields, or dense interfaces. Use sparingly and ensure sufficient contrast, as this size approaches minimum readability thresholds for some users.
      </p>

      <p>To set helper text in size XS, you can use the <code>.helper-xs</code> class:</p>

      <div className="code-example">
        <Source dark format="dedent" language="html" code={`<span class="helper-xs">${SampleText}</span>`} />
      </div>
      <Typeset
        fontFamily={`"${getCurrentFont().name}", ${getCurrentFont().fallbacks}`}
        fontSizes={[parseInt(TYPOGRAPHY.xs)]}
        fontWeight={400}
        sampleText={SampleText}
      />

      <h4>Label (L)</h4>
      <p>
        Label L (20px) is used for prominent form labels, section identifiers, or interface elements requiring enhanced visibility. At 20px with semibold weight, these labels maintain strong presence while sitting comfortably within the typographic hierarchy. Use for important categorization or when labels need to stand out.
      </p>

      <p>To set a label in size L, you can use the <code>.label-l</code> class:</p>

      <div className="code-example">
        <Source dark format="dedent" language="html" code={`<label class="label-l">${SampleText}</label>`} />
      </div>
      <Typeset
        fontFamily={`"${getCurrentFont().name}", ${getCurrentFont().fallbacks}`}
        fontSizes={[parseInt(TYPOGRAPHY.l)]}
        fontWeight={600}
        sampleText={SampleText}
      />

      <h4>Label (M)</h4>
      <p>
        Label M (16px) is our standard size for form labels, providing good visibility while integrating harmoniously with surrounding content. The semibold weight ensures emphasis without dominating the interface. This versatile size works well across most contexts and maintains readability on various screen sizes.
      </p>

      <p>To set a label in size M, you can use the <code>.label-m</code> class:</p>

      <div className="code-example">
        <Source dark format="dedent" language="html" code={`<label class="label-m">${SampleText}</label>`} />
      </div>
      <Typeset
        fontFamily={`"${getCurrentFont().name}", ${getCurrentFont().fallbacks}`}
        fontSizes={[parseInt(TYPOGRAPHY.m)]}
        fontWeight={600}
        sampleText={SampleText}
      />

      <h4>Label (S)</h4>
      <p>
        Label S (14px) offers compact labeling for space-constrained interfaces or secondary form elements. The semibold weight ensures readability despite the smaller size. Use for field groups, dense forms, or when multiple labels need to coexist in a limited space.
      </p>

      <p>To set a label in size S, you can use the <code>.label-s</code> class:</p>

      <div className="code-example">
        <Source dark format="dedent" language="html" code={`<label class="label-s">${SampleText}</label>`} />
      </div>
      <Typeset
        fontFamily={`"${getCurrentFont().name}", ${getCurrentFont().fallbacks}`}
        fontSizes={[parseInt(TYPOGRAPHY.s)]}
        fontWeight={600}
        sampleText={SampleText}
      />

      <h4>Label (XS)</h4>
      <p>
        Label XS (12px) is our most compact label size, used for tight spaces, badges, tags, or micro-interactions. The semibold weight compensates for the small size to maintain legibility. Use sparingly and with high contrast backgrounds to ensure accessibility.
      </p>

      <p>To set a label in size XS, you can use the <code>.label-xs</code> class:</p>

      <div className="code-example">
        <Source dark format="dedent" language="html" code={`<label class="label-xs">${SampleText}</label>`} />
      </div>
      <Typeset
        fontFamily={`"${getCurrentFont().name}", ${getCurrentFont().fallbacks}`}
        fontSizes={[parseInt(TYPOGRAPHY.xs)]}
        fontWeight={600}
        sampleText={SampleText}
      />

      <h4>Code (S)</h4>
      <p>
        Code S (14px) is designed for inline code snippets, commands, or technical notations within regular text. Using a monospace font at 14px with regular weight, it provides clear distinction for code elements while maintaining visual harmony with surrounding content. This style enhances technical documentation by clearly identifying programmatic content.
      </p>

      <p>To set code text in size S, you can use the native <code>code</code> HTML tag:</p>

      <div className="code-example">
        <Source dark format="dedent" language="html" code={`<code>${SampleText}</code>`} />
      </div>

      <p>or the <code>.code</code>class:</p>

      <div className="code-example">
        <Source dark format="dedent" language="html" code={`<p class="code">${SampleText}</p>`} />
      </div>

      <Typeset
        fontFamily={`"${getCurrentFont('secondary').name}", ${getCurrentFont('secondary').fallbacks}`}
        fontSizes={[parseInt(TYPOGRAPHY.s)]}
        fontWeight={400}
        sampleText={SampleText}
      />

      <h4>Underlined (M)</h4>
      <p>
        Underlined M (16px) is used for text that requires emphasis or indicates interactivity, such as links within paragraph content. At our standard 16px size with regular weight and underline decoration, it maintains readability while providing clear visual distinction for interactive or emphasized content.
      </p>

      <p>To set underlined text in size M, you can use the <code>.underline-m</code> class:</p>

      <div className="code-example">
        <Source dark format="dedent" language="html" code={`<span class="underline-m">${SampleText}</span>`} />
      </div>
      <Typeset
        fontFamily={`"${getCurrentFont().name}", ${getCurrentFont().fallbacks}`}
        fontSizes={[parseInt(TYPOGRAPHY.m)]}
        fontWeight={400}
        sampleText={SampleText}
        style={{ textDecoration: "underline" }}
      />

      <h4>Underlined (S)</h4>
      <p>
        Underlined S (14px) provides a more compact option for emphasized or interactive text in space-constrained layouts. The smaller size integrates well with supporting content while the underline decoration ensures it remains distinct and recognizable as interactive content.
      </p>

      <p>To set underlined text in size S, you can use the <code>.underline-s</code> class:</p>

      <div className="code-example">
        <Source dark format="dedent" language="html" code={`<span class="underline-s">${SampleText}</span>`} />
      </div>
      <Typeset
        fontFamily={`"${getCurrentFont().name}", ${getCurrentFont().fallbacks}`}
        fontSizes={[parseInt(TYPOGRAPHY.s)]}
        fontWeight={400}
        sampleText={SampleText}
        style={{ textDecoration: "underline" }}
      />

      <h4>Underlined (XS)</h4>
      <p>
        Underlined XS (12px) is our smallest underlined text style, used for compact UI elements, footnote links, or secondary navigation items where space is extremely limited. The underline decoration ensures interactivity cues remain clear despite the reduced size.
      </p>

      <p>To set underlined text in size XS, you can use the <code>.underline-xs</code> class:</p>

      <div className="code-example">
        <Source dark format="dedent" language="html" code={`<span class="underline-xs">${SampleText}</span>`} />
      </div>
      <Typeset
        fontFamily={`"${getCurrentFont().name}", ${getCurrentFont().fallbacks}`}
        fontSizes={[parseInt(TYPOGRAPHY.xs)]}
        fontWeight={400}
        sampleText={SampleText}
        style={{ textDecoration: "underline" }}
      />
    </div>

  </div>
</div>
